        - start_line = 1
        - splitter_class = DefaultSplitter
        - position_char = "="
      - Methods:
        - get_fixedwidth_params(self, line)
          - Calls super().get_fixedwidth_params(line)
          - Mutation: sets ends[-1] = None (rightmost column unbounded)
    - SimpleRSTData(FixedWidthData)
      - Class attributes:
        - start_line = 3
        - end_line = -1
        - splitter_class = FixedWidthTwoLineDataSplitter
      - No custom methods in this class; uses FixedWidthData.write / BasicData behavior.
    - RST(FixedWidth)
      - Class-level attributes:
        - _format_name = "rst"
        - _description = "reStructuredText simple table"
        - data_class = SimpleRSTData
        - header_class = SimpleRSTHeader
      - Methods:
        - __init__(self)
          - Calls super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False)
          - Note: no header_rows parameter accepted or forwarded
        - write(self, lines)
          - Calls lines = super().write(lines)
          - Then does: lines = [lines[1]] + lines + [lines[1]] and returns lines
            - This hardcodes use of lines[1] as the border line that will be used as top/bottom border of the reST simple table.

- What this implies:
  - The immediate cause of the TypeError in the report is that core._get_writer is passing header_rows in writer kwargs and RST.__init__ has no header_rows parameter — Python raises TypeError.
  - Any fix must consider how RST interacts with FixedWidth header/data machinery, and how its write() behavior (using lines[1]) depends on header_rows assumptions.

2) Inspect component / associated classes (from fixedwidth.py)
- I fetched astropy/io/ascii/fixedwidth.py and examined the relevant classes and logic:

  - FixedWidth class (inherits basic.Basic)
    - __init__(self, col_starts=None, col_ends=None, delimiter_pad=" ", bookend=True, header_rows=None)
      - If header_rows is None => header_rows = ["name"]
      - Sets various attributes:
        - self.data.splitter.delimiter_pad = delimiter_pad
        - self.data.splitter.bookend = bookend
        - self.header.col_starts = col_starts
        - self.header.col_ends = col_ends
        - self.header.header_rows = header_rows
        - self.data.header_rows = header_rows
        - if self.data.start_line is None:
            self.data.start_line = len(header_rows)
    - header_class = FixedWidthHeader
    - data_class = FixedWidthData

  - FixedWidthHeader(basic.BasicHeader)
    - Attributes:
      - splitter_class = FixedWidthHeaderSplitter
      - position_line = None
      - set_of_position_line_characters = {...}
    - Methods:
      - get_line(self, lines, index): returns the requested line from process_lines(lines)
      - get_cols(self, lines): complex logic to initialize columns
        - header_rows = getattr(self, "header_rows", ["name"])
        - computes start_line and position_line using core._get_line_index
        - handles cases where position_line provided / not provided (single header line vs two-line header)
        - obtains the header name line via:
            line = self.get_line(lines, start_line + header_rows.index("name"))
          (i.e., it expects "name" to be in header_rows and uses its index to find which header row contains the column names)
        - For each extra attr in header_rows (attributes other than "name"), it sets attributes on columns using:
            for ii, attr in enumerate(header_rows):
                if attr != "name":
                    line = self.get_line(lines, start_line + ii)
                    vals = self.get_fixedwidth_params(line)[0]
                    set attribute attr on each column if val present
      - get_fixedwidth_params(self, line): returns vals, starts, ends (used by SimpleRSTHeader.get_fixedwidth_params too)
      - write(self, lines): pass (header writing postponed until data widths known in data.write)

  - FixedWidthData(basic.BasicData)
    - Attributes:
      - splitter_class = FixedWidthSplitter
      - start_line = None (default)
    - Methods:
      - write(self, lines)
        - default_header_rows = [] if header.start_line is None else ["name"]
        - header_rows = getattr(self, "header_rows", default_header_rows)
        - builds vals_list from data columns
        - builds hdrs_list from header_rows by reading col.info.<attr> for each column
        - computes column widths from data and hdrs_list
        - writes rows for header rows: for vals in hdrs_list: lines.append(self.splitter.join(vals, widths))
        - if self.header.position_line is not None:
            append a position_line row built from self.header.position_char * width for each column
        - writes data lines using splitter.join
        - returns lines

    - FixedWidthTwoLine.__init__ accepts header_rows and forwards to super().__init__(..., header_rows=header_rows)
    - Also sets header.position_line and data.start_line based on header_rows length if position_line is None

- What this implies:
  - FixedWidth has an established pattern: it accepts header_rows, sets header.header_rows and data.header_rows, and (if data.start_line is None) sets data.start_line = len(header_rows).
  - Other FixedWidth-derived classes that want header_rows behavior accept header_rows in their __init__ and forward to super(); FixedWidthTwoLine is an example of that pattern.
  - RST does not accept header_rows and therefore cannot receive header_rows from core._get_writer — leading to the TypeError.

3) Inspect class hierarchy and inherited behavior
- Basic writer machinery (basic.py):
  - Basic class sets header_class and data_class. Basic and BasicHeader/BasicData provide default start_line values and the common writing framework.
  - FixedWidth uses FixedWidthHeader and FixedWidthData which override splitting/joining behavior appropriate for fixed-width style.
  - RST subclasses FixedWidth, so it inherits all the FixedWidth header/data machinery unless it overrides or adjusts things.

- core._get_writer (astropy/io/ascii/core.py):
  - _get_writer collects writer kwargs and constructs writer = Writer(**writer_kwargs) where writer_kwargs is a dict of kwargs that are not in extra_writer_pars.
  - header_rows is not in extra_writer_pars, so header_rows will be passed into Writer(**writer_kwargs) — which is why header_rows attempted to call RST.__init__ with header_rows.
  - Additional code then sets writer.header.splitter.delimiter and other attributes from kwargs.

- What this implies:
  - The writer factory intentionally passes header_rows to writer constructors. Writers that expect header_rows should accept it. RST currently does not, so the TypeError is expected.
  - Any change to RST.__init__ to accept header_rows must be consistent with how FixedWidth and other subclasses handle header_rows. We must also confirm the rest of RST write logic works when header_rows length varies.

4) Trace all operations on the same data (header_rows, position_line, start_line)
- FixedWidthHeader.get_cols reads header_rows and uses header_rows.index("name") to find the name row.
- FixedWidthData.write uses header_rows to assemble header rows, then uses header.position_line to produce the separation line.
- SimpleRSTHeader sets position_line = 0 and start_line = 1 (so by default it expects name to be at start_line + header_rows.index("name") — with default header_rows = ["name"], that yields the single name line at index 1).
- SimpleRSTData has start_line = 3 (fixed), which encodes the default layout for the RST simple table format:
  - Interpreting the default: header block lines are expected at lines 1..(1 + n_header_rows - 1), position_line at line 0 etc., and the data.start_line = 3 indicates data begins after borders/headers as RST expects.
- RST.write takes the lines produced by FixedWidth.write and then wraps them by copying lines[1] to top and bottom: lines = [lines[1]] + lines + [lines[1]]
  - This relies on a specific layout of lines returned from the FixedWidth/data/header write process.
  - If header_rows length changes, the index of the border line (lines[1]) that RST relies on may change — so write() behavior could be incorrect for header_rows != default.

- What this implies:
  - Accepting header_rows must be coordinated with these hardcoded row indices in the RST-related classes to avoid misplacing the border lines.
  - There are two possible areas to pay attention to when adding header_rows support:
    1. The RST constructor needs to accept header_rows so FixedWidth machinery can attach header_rows to header and data objects.
    2. RST.write's use of lines[1] as the border should be re-evaluated for robustness against varying header_rows lengths (it may need a dynamic calculation based on header.header_rows and header.position_line).

5) Callers and consumers
- core._get_writer is the place that passes header_rows in writer kwargs (and the path the stack trace shows).
- Tests: fixedwidth tests exercise header_rows in multiple formats (fixed_width, fixed_width_two_line), and there is an explicit test confirming that fixed_width_no_header raises TypeError when header_rows is provided (because that specific class intentionally should not accept header_rows).
- There are no existing tests for ascii.rst writer with header_rows (i.e., no regression tests in the repo for this specific case). The bug report demonstrates an actual user scenario not currently tested.

- I searched for other classes that subclass FixedWidth. Most either:

- RST is the only FixedWidth-derived writer in the codebase that overrides __init__ and does not accept header_rows.

      - Then wraps output using lines = [lines[1]] + lines + [lines[1]]
      - Hardcoded use of lines[1] as the border/top-bottom delimiter line; this index assumes a specific layout of the output when header_rows is default.

- Component classes:
  - SimpleRSTHeader(FixedWidthHeader)
    - position_line = 0
    - start_line = 1
    - position_char = "="
    - get_fixedwidth_params(self, line) tweaks ends[-1] = None to make rightmost column unbounded
    - Uses FixedWidthHeader.get_cols logic that relies on header_rows to determine which header row contains the "name" and which rows contain attributes like "unit"
  - SimpleRSTData(FixedWidthData)
    - start_line = 3
    - end_line = -1
    - splitter_class = FixedWidthTwoLineDataSplitter
    - Uses FixedWidthData.write for header/data formatting; FixedWidthData.write uses header_rows to construct hdrs_list and header.position_line to insert position line.

- Parent class (FixedWidth), key methods/attributes that RST inherits:
  - __init__(..., header_rows=None): default header_rows = ["name"] if None
    - Sets:
      - self.header.header_rows = header_rows
      - self.data.header_rows = header_rows
      - If self.data.start_line is None: self.data.start_line = len(header_rows)
    - Also sets self.data.splitter.delimiter_pad and self.data.splitter.bookend from constructor args
  - FixedWidthHeader.get_cols
    - Uses header_rows to find name line: start_line + header_rows.index("name")
    - For each attr in header_rows (except "name"), sets attributes (e.g., unit) on columns from header rows
  - FixedWidthData.write
    - Builds header rows according to self.data.header_rows (uses col.info.<attr> values)
    - Inserts position_line (if set) as a line of repeated header.position_char across widths
    - Writes data rows; ordering of output lines depends on header_rows and position_line

- Sibling classes / examples:
  - FixedWidthTwoLine: accepts header_rows in its __init__ and forwards header_rows to FixedWidth.__init__. Then sets header.position_line and data.start_line appropriately. This shows the established pattern used by other fixed-width writers that support multiple header rows.

- All code paths that touch the same data:
  - core._get_writer collects writer kwargs and passes header_rows to the Writer constructor (so RST.__init__ is invoked with header_rows if header_rows is supplied).
  - FixedWidth.__init__ propagates header_rows to header and data objects, and programmatically sets data.start_line only if data.start_line is None.
  - FixedWidthHeader.get_cols reads header_rows to find name row and other attribute rows.
  - FixedWidthData.write uses header_rows to build hdrs_list, inserts position_line if header.position_line is not None, then writes data lines.

Hardcoded values / constants that encode assumptions about current behavior
- SimpleRSTHeader.position_line = 0 and start_line = 1 — these encode the expected offsets for the RST simple-table header block and its position/formatting row.
- SimpleRSTData.start_line = 3 — this is a fixed start line for RST data; it assumes a particular number/ordering of lines in the RST simple table format (including the top border added by RST.write).
- RST.write uses lines[1] to compute the border line to be added above and below the table — an index that assumes a particular output layout from FixedWidthData.write and is sensitive to header_rows and header.position_line arrangements.

Concrete fault location and its effects
- The immediate TypeError arises because RST.__init__ does not accept header_rows keyword, while core._get_writer passes header_rows along to writer constructors. So writer initialization fails for RST when header_rows is provided.
- The broader set of code that will be affected by enabling header_rows for RST:
  - RST.__init__ (must accept and integrate header_rows into its configuration)
  - RST.write (current use of lines[1] may be brittle if header_rows length changes; it may need to compute which line in the output is the border dynamically)
  - SimpleRSTData.start_line = 3 (class attribute) — because FixedWidth.__init__ sets data.start_line only when it was None, RST’s current hardcoded start_line will prevent FixedWidth from adjusting data.start_line based on a different header_rows length supplied by the user; this might cause layout mismatches when header_rows length differs from the RST assumptions.

Tests and callsites to update/add
- There are existing tests covering header_rows for fixed_width and fixed_width_two_line, but no tests exercising ascii.rst writer with header_rows. New tests should exercise:
  - Writing an RST table with header_rows=["name", "unit"] (the user example) and assert the produced reStructuredText table lines match expected layout (including the top/bottom border and unit row).
  - Possibly a read/write round-trip for RST with header_rows of length 1 and length 2 to confirm parsing/writing symmetry.
- core._get_writer already passes header_rows for all writer formats; tests should confirm that only formats that deliberately don't support header_rows continue to raise TypeError (as current test shows for fixed_width_no_header).

Gaps / risk areas to investigate (do not change yet; these are places to examine when implementing a fix)
- SimpleRSTData.start_line is hardcoded to 3, so FixedWidth.__init__ will not change it even if header_rows of a different length are supplied. Evaluate whether data.start_line should be dependent on header_rows for RST when the user supplies header_rows of different length.
- RST.write uses a fixed index lines[1] for the border. Confirm whether a dynamic selection (based on header.header_rows and header.position_line) is required so the top and bottom borders are correct for variable-length header_rows.
- FixedWidthHeader.get_cols uses header_rows.index("name") to locate the column name row — this requires that header_rows always contain "name". If writer kwargs allow header_rows without "name", that will raise ValueError. Check whether callers can pass header_rows lists that omit "name" (core or docs probably expect "name" to be present), and safeguard or validate accordingly.
- Verify other writers that subclass FixedWidth do not have similar omissions: my search found RST as the sole FixedWidth subclass that overrides __init__ without header_rows. Other fixed-width derived classes follow the established pattern.

Suggested next steps for implementer (actionable checklist for the fix phase)
- Ensure RST.__init__ accepts header_rows in its signature and propagates it consistently to the FixedWidth initialization path, following the established pattern used by other FixedWidth subclasses.
- Review and, if necessary, adjust RST.write so that it determines the correct border/top-bottom line dynamically (rather than hardcoding lines[1]) once header_rows support is enabled.
- Decide whether SimpleRSTData.start_line should be left as-is (class-level default) or be allowed to change based on user-supplied header_rows (i.e., set data.start_line relative to header_rows length when header_rows passed explicitly).
- Add unit tests for ascii.rst writer with header_rows (e.g., ["name", "unit"]) to assert the expected output layout and prevent regressions.
- Run the full ascii writer/reader test suite to ensure no other writer is broken by the change.

Thought: I will now analyze what super().write(lines) returns for FixedWidth subclasses. Based on FixedWidth.write in basic.BaseReader, write() calls write_header(lines, meta) then self.data.write(lines). write_header calls header.write_comments then header.write(lines). For FixedWidth, FixedWidthHeader.write is pass (it defers writing until data.write).     - self.write_header(lines, table.meta) -> header.write_comments and header.write(lines)
    - self.data.write(lines)
    - return lines
  - So the writer’s header.write and data.write together build the lines list in the order header comments, header rows, position row (if any), then data rows. The position row is appended by data.write (FixedWidthData.write), not header.write, for fixed-width style formats.
- What this implies:
  - The index of the position line inside the list returned by super().write(lines) depends on how many header rows (header_rows) were appended by data.write earlier. Therefore RST.write should compute that index dynamically (based on header.header_rows) instead of hardcoding lines[1].

Step: Inspect FixedWidth writer implementation to see exactly how header_rows and position_line are written
- Why: The RST writer subclasses FixedWidth. Understanding FixedWidth.__init__, FixedWidthHeader, and FixedWidthData.write shows how header_rows are stored and used and how the resulting lines list looks.
- Tool call: get_file_context(astropy/io/ascii/fixedwidth.py)
- What I looked for: FixedWidth.__init__, FixedWidthHeader.get_cols/position_line semantics, FixedWidthData.write, and how header_rows are stored/propagated.
- What I found (key details):
  - FixedWidth.__init__(..., header_rows=None):
    - If header_rows is None, default to ["name"].
    - Calls super().__init__().
    - Sets splitter.delimiter_pad, splitter.bookend, header.col_starts/col_ends, header.header_rows, data.header_rows.
    - If self.data.start_line is None: self.data.start_line = len(header_rows)
  - FixedWidthHeader:
    - Attributes: position_line = None, set_of_position_line_characters defined.
    - get_cols(self, lines) uses:
      - header_rows = getattr(self, "header_rows", ["name"])
      - start_line = _get_line_index(self.start_line, self.process_lines(lines))
      - position_line = _get_line_index(self.position_line, self.process_lines(lines))
      - If position_line not None, it uses that line to compute column starts/ends. It finds the name line via get_line(lines, start_line + header_rows.index("name")) and fills other header attributes by iterating header_rows.
  - FixedWidthData.write(self, lines):
    - header_rows = getattr(self, "header_rows", default_header_rows) where default_header_rows = [] if header.start_line is None else ["name"]
    - It builds hdrs_list for each header attribute (e.g., "name", "unit", etc.) and appends to lines in that order
    - If self.header.position_line is not None: append the position_line (composed of header.position_char repeated per column width)
    - Then append formatted data rows
    - Returns lines
- What I learned and implications:
  - FixedWidth provides the intended mechanism to accept header_rows via its constructor and write them, appending first header rows then the position_line (if any). The position_line will appear at index len(header_rows) in the list returned by data.write (assuming hdrs_list length equals len(header_rows)).
  - FixedWidth.__init__ sets both header.header_rows and data.header_rows, and sets data.start_line = len(header_rows) only if data.start_line was previously None. If a subclass (like RST) hard-codes data.start_line to a non-None value, FixedWidth.__init__ will not override it. This may matter for read behavior with non-default header_rows.

Therefore RST.write must be changed to compute the correct index instead of using lines[1]. This is a secondary change that must be included in the full fix.

- Why: Confirm that the code which selects and initializes writers passes header_rows to the Writer, creating the TypeError when the Writer's __init__ does not accept header_rows.

1) RST.__init__ doesn't accept header_rows (primary cause of the TypeError)

2) RST.write uses a hardcoded index for the border/position line (lines[1])
- Location: astropy/io/ascii/rst.py: RST.write
- What I found: FixedWidthData.write returns lines in this order:
    - header rows (hdrs_list, length = len(header_rows))
    - position line (if header.position_line is not None)
    - data rows
  So the position/border line will be at index = len(header_rows) (if position_line exists).
- Implication: change RST.write to compute the position index dynamically. For example:
    lines = super().write(lines)
    if self.header.position_line is not None:
        pos_index = len(getattr(self.header, "header_rows", ["name"]))
        border_line = lines[pos_index]
    else:

        border_line = lines[0] if lines else ""
    lines = [border_line] + lines + [border_line]
    return lines
  This avoids assuming header_rows length = 1.

3) SimpleRSTData.start_line is hard-coded to 3 (class attribute)
- Location: astropy/io/ascii/rst.py: class SimpleRSTData
- What I found: FixedWidth.__init__ would set self.data.start_line = len(header_rows) only if self.data.start_line is None. SimpleRSTData.start_line = 3 (not None), so FixedWidth.__init__ will not override it.
- Implications to check:
  - RST reading behavior with custom header_rows: if you allow header_rows length != expected (e.g., len=2), does SimpleRSTData.start_line == 3 still correctly select data rows? RST writing will add top border and bottom border lines in addition to the header rows and position line; RST.read must find header and data using its start_line/position_line values. The existing pattern apparently encodes the offsets of the border lines relative to header lines for reading; forwarding header_rows to FixedWidth.__init__ will cause header.header_rows and data.header_rows to be set, but data.start_line will remain 3. For typical header_rows=["name","unit"] this may be OK, but this should be validated by tests. If tests show misalignment, modify SimpleRSTData or RST.__init__ to set data.start_line dynamically when header_rows provided (e.g., set self.data.start_line = len(header_rows) + 1 or similar) — but do not change this without tests. - What I found: position_line = 0, start_line = 1. These are consistent with the reST simple table format which has top border, header name, position_line, data..., bottom border. When header_rows vary, the mapping may need adjusting or more robust computation, but FixedWidthHeader.get_cols uses start_line + header_rows.index("name") to find the name row and iterates over header_rows to set attributes, so the code already supports multiple header_rows when reading. If needed, adjust start_line or add logic in RST.__init__ to update header.position_line/start_line/data.start_line when a custom header_rows is passed.

         border (position_char line)
         name row (start_line 1)
         position row (position_line 0 relative to start_line)
         data rows (start at index 3)
         border (position_char line)

- Parent class: FixedWidth (astropy/io/ascii/fixedwidth.py)
  - FixedWidth.__init__(col_starts=None, col_ends=None, delimiter_pad=" ", bookend=True, header_rows=None):

- Callers and consumers:

  - RST.write uses lines[1] hardcoded (assumes header_rows length = 1 and position_line present at index 1)

proposed_fix.change:
Change signature to accept header_rows (default None) and forward it to FixedWidth.__init__. After calling super(), if header_rows is not None, update the data start-line to match the provided header_rows layout (so data rows are placed at the correct offset). Example implementation logic:

def __init__(self, header_rows=None):
    super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False, header_rows=header_rows)
    # If the caller explicitly provided header_rows, ensure the data start_line
    # aligns with the number of header rows plus the position line if present.
    if header_rows is not None:
        pos_present = getattr(self.header, 'position_line', None) is not None
        self.data.start_line = len(header_rows) + (1 if pos_present else 0)
proposed_fix.reason: core._get_writer passes header_rows through writer kwargs; FixedWidth already supports header_rows when forwarded. RST must accept/forward header_rows so the caller does not get a TypeError and so FixedWidth will populate header.header_rows/data.header_rows. Updating data.start_line when header_rows is explicitly provided ensures the data rows are written at the correct offset for RST's layout.

proposed_fix.change:
Stop hardcoding lines[1] as the border/position line. Compute the index of the position (border) line dynamically from header.header_rows and header.position_line, then use that index to pick the border line to prepend/append. Example logic:

def write(self, lines):
    lines = super().write(lines)
    # Determine header_rows length as written by FixedWidthData.write
    header_rows = getattr(self.header, 'header_rows', ['name'])
    if getattr(self.header, 'position_line', None) is not None:
        pos_index = len(header_rows)  # position line follows header rows
        border = lines[pos_index]
        lines = [border] + lines + [border]
    # else: leave lines unchanged (or decide desired behavior)
    return lines

bug_report.Title: ascii.rst writer: TypeError on header_rows and incorrect border placement for multi-row headers
bug_report.Description: When calling the RestructuredText (ascii.rst) writer with header_rows provided (for example header_rows=["name","unit"]) the writer raises a TypeError because RST.__init__ does not accept header_rows and therefore does not forward it to the FixedWidth base class. In addition, the RST.write implementation assumes the border/position line is always at index 1 in the list of lines returned by the base writer; this breaks when header_rows length != 1 and produces incorrect table borders. Tests covering ascii.rst with header_rows are missing.
bug_report.RootCause: 1) RST.__init__ has no header_rows parameter and does not forward header_rows to FixedWidth.__init__, causing a TypeError when core._get_writer passes header_rows through writer kwargs. 2) RST.write unconditionally uses lines[1] as the border/position line; that index is only correct for the default single header row. 3) SimpleRSTData.start_line is a hard-coded class attribute (3), so FixedWidth.__init__ will not override it when header_rows is supplied; this means data start offsets won't automatically adapt for non-default header_rows unless RST adjusts data.start_line when header_rows is explicitly provided. 4) There are no tests asserting ascii.rst writing/reading with header_rows variations.
StepsToReproduce: Run the Python snippet from the original report:
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: The ascii.rst writer should accept header_rows (default None) and forward it to FixedWidth so header/header_rows and data.header_rows are set consistently. When header_rows is provided (e.g. ['name','unit']), the writer should produce a valid reStructuredText simple-table with both name and unit header rows, with the top/bottom border (position line) placed at the correct index relative to the header rows. No TypeError should be raised.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Calling tbl.write(..., format='ascii.rst', header_rows=[...]) raises TypeError: RST.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'header_rows'. Even after addressing the TypeError, produced border lines may be placed incorrectly when header_rows length != 1 because RST.write assumes a fixed index for the border line.
bug_report.Suggestions: 1) Modify RST.__init__ to accept header_rows=None and forward header_rows to FixedWidth.__init__. 2) After calling super().__init__, if the caller explicitly provided header_rows, update self.data.start_line so data rows start at the expected offset relative to the number of header rows and presence of a position_line. 3) Update RST.write to compute the index of the border/position line dynamically from header.header_rows and header.position_line (instead of using lines[1]). 4) Add unit tests in astropy/io/ascii/tests/test_rst.py that exercise writing (and read/write round-trips) with header_rows=['name','unit'] (including the QTable+units example) and assert the produced reStructuredText simple-table matches the expected layout. 5) Review SimpleRSTHeader.position_line and start_line offsets via tests to ensure read/write symmetry for non-default header_rows; adjust if tests show mismatches. These changes are minimal, align RST with other FixedWidth subclasses, and prevent TypeError and malformed output for multi-row headers.
bug_report.possible_fix: 1) Change RST.__init__ signature to accept header_rows=None and forward header_rows to FixedWidth.__init__. After calling super(), if header_rows is not None, update self.data.start_line so the data rows are placed at the correct offset relative to the header rows and any position_line. 2) Change RST.write to compute the border (position) line index dynamically using the length of header.header_rows and header.position_line presence rather than hardcoding lines[1]. 3) Add unit tests in astropy/io/ascii/tests/test_rst.py to exercise writing and read/write round-trips for header_rows=['name','unit'] (including QTable with units). These unit tests should lock down desired behavior and will reveal if SimpleRSTHeader position/start offsets need adjustment; if they do, refine header.position_line/start_line or RST.__init__ logic accordingly.

possible_fix_code.astropy.io.ascii.rst.RST.__init__:
def __init__(self, header_rows=None):
    """Initialize the RST writer.

    Accept and forward header_rows to the FixedWidth base class. If the
    caller explicitly provided header_rows, ensure the data.start_line is set
    so that data rows are placed at the correct offset (header rows + position
    line if present).
    """
    # Forward header_rows to FixedWidth so it populates header.header_rows
    # and data.header_rows. Keep the RST-specific options delimiter_pad and
    # bookend as before.
    super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False, header_rows=header_rows)

    # If caller explicitly provided header_rows, adjust data.start_line so the
    # data rows start immediately after the header rows and the optional
    # position_line. FixedWidth.__init__ only adjusts data.start_line when
    # data.start_line is None; SimpleRSTData defines a class default that would
    # prevent that. To support explicit header_rows we update data.start_line
    # here so that RST writes place data at the intended offset.
    if header_rows is not None:
        pos_present = getattr(self.header, 'position_line', None) is not None
        # position_line (if present) is typically a single line between header
        # rows and data (e.g. the ===== border). Add 1 when present.
        self.data.start_line = len(header_rows) + (1 if pos_present else 0)
possible_fix_code.astropy.io.ascii.rst.RST.write:
def write(self, lines):
    """Write the lines produced by the base FixedWidth writer and add the
    outer RST borders in the correct place for arbitrary header_rows.

    The base writer returns a list of lines where the header rows (as
    specified by header.header_rows) are written first, followed by an
    optional position_line (border) and then data lines. Previously RST.write
    assumed the border was always at index 1; for header_rows lengths != 1
    this is incorrect. Here compute the index dynamically and use that
    border line to prepend/append the top/bottom borders.
    """
    lines = super().write(lines)

    # Determine header_rows as actually used by the header object (FixedWidth
    # sets header.header_rows). Fall back to ['name'] to be conservative.
    header_rows = getattr(self.header, 'header_rows', ['name'])

    # If the header defines a position_line, the position/border line in the
    # output follows the header_rows and therefore is at index len(header_rows)
    # in the list of lines returned by the base writer. Use that index to pick
    # the border and add it as the outer top and bottom border. If there is no
    # position_line defined, leave lines unchanged.
    if getattr(self.header, 'position_line', None) is not None:
        pos_index = len(header_rows)
        # Safety check: only operate if pos_index is within the produced lines
        if 0 <= pos_index < len(lines):
            border = lines[pos_index]
            lines = [border] + lines + [border]
    return lines

method_cache.astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py:_line_type:
def _line_type(line, delimiter=None):
    """Interpret a QDP file line.

    Parameters
    ----------
    line : str
        a single line of the file

    Returns
    -------
    type : str
        Line type: "comment", "command", or "data"

    Examples
    --------
    >>> _line_type("READ SERR 3")
    'command'
    >>> _line_type(" \\n    !some gibberish")
    'comment'
    >>> _line_type("   ")
    'comment'
    >>> _line_type(" 21345.45")
    'data,1'
    >>> _line_type(" 21345.45 1.53e-3 1e-3 .04 NO nan")
    'data,6'
    >>> _line_type(" 21345.45,1.53e-3,1e-3,.04,NO,nan", delimiter=',')
    'data,6'
    >>> _line_type(" 21345.45 ! a comment to disturb")
    'data,1'
    >>> _line_type("NO NO NO NO NO")
    'new'
    >>> _line_type("NO,NO,NO,NO,NO", delimiter=',')
    'new'
    >>> _line_type("N O N NOON OON O")
    Traceback (most recent call last):
        ...
    ValueError: Unrecognized QDP line...
    >>> _line_type(" some non-comment gibberish")
    Traceback (most recent call last):
        ...
    ValueError: Unrecognized QDP line...
    """
    _decimal_re = r"[+-]?(\d+(\.\d*)?|\.\d+)([eE][+-]?\d+)?"
    _command_re = r"READ [TS]ERR(\s+[0-9]+)+"

    sep = delimiter
    if delimiter is None:
        sep = r"\s+"
    _new_re = rf"NO({sep}NO)+"
    _data_re = rf"({_decimal_re}|NO|[-+]?nan)({sep}({_decimal_re}|NO|[-+]?nan))*)"
    _type_re = rf"^\s*((?P<command>{_command_re})|(?P<new>{_new_re})|(?P<data>{_data_re})?\s*(\!(?P<comment>.*))?\s*$"
    _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re)
    line = line.strip()
    if not line:
        return "comment"
    match = _line_type_re.match(line)

    if match is None:
        raise ValueError(f"Unrecognized QDP line: {line}")
    for type_, val in match.groupdict().items():
        if val is None:
            continue
        if type_ == "data":
            return f"data,{len(val.split(sep=delimiter))}"
        else:
            return type_
method_cache.astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py:_get_tables_from:
def _get_tables_from_qdp_file(qdp_file, input_colnames=None, delimiter=None):
    """Get all tables from a QDP file.

    Parameters
    ----------
    qdp_file : str
        Input QDP file name

    Other Parameters
    ----------------
    input_colnames : list of str
        Name of data columns (defaults to ['col1', 'col2', ...]), _not_
        including error columns.
    delimiter : str
        Delimiter for the values in the table.

    Returns
    -------
    list of `~astropy.table.Table`
        List containing all the tables present inside the QDP file
    """
    lines = _get_lines_from_file(qdp_file)
    contents, ncol = _get_type_from_list_of_lines(lines, delimiter=delimiter)

    table_list = []
    err_specs = {}
    colnames = None

    comment_text = ""
    initial_comments = ""
    command_lines = ""
    current_rows = None

    for line, datatype in zip(lines, contents):
        line = line.strip().lstrip("!")
        # Is this a comment?
        if datatype == "comment":
            comment_text += line + "\n"
            continue

        if datatype == "command":
            # The first time I find commands, I save whatever comments into
            # The initial comments.
            if command_lines == "":
                initial_comments = comment_text
                comment_text = ""

            if err_specs != {}:
                warnings.warn(
                    "This file contains multiple command blocks. Please verify",
                    AstropyUserWarning,
                )
            command_lines += line + "\n"
            continue

        if datatype.startswith("data"):
            # The first time I find data, I define err_specs
            if err_specs == {} and command_lines != "":
                for cline in command_lines.strip().split("\n"):
                    command = cline.strip().split()
                    # This should never happen, but just in case.
                    if len(command) < 3:
                        continue
                    err_specs[command[1].lower()] = [int(c) for c in command[2:]]
            if colnames is None:
                colnames = _interpret_err_lines(err_specs, ncol, names=input_colnames)

            if current_rows is None:
                current_rows = []

            values = []
            for v in line.split(delimiter):
                if v == "NO":
                    values.append(np.ma.masked)
                else:
                    # Understand if number is int or float
                    try:
                        values.append(int(v))
                    except ValueError:
                        values.append(float(v))
            current_rows.append(values)
            continue

        if datatype == "new":
            # Save table to table_list and reset
            if current_rows is not None:
                new_table = Table(names=colnames, rows=current_rows)
                new_table.meta["initial_comments"] = initial_comments.strip().split(
                    "\n"
                )
                new_table.meta["comments"] = comment_text.strip().split("\n")
                # Reset comments
                comment_text = ""
                table_list.append(new_table)
                current_rows = None
            continue

    # At the very end, if there is still a table being written, let's save
    # it to the table_list
    if current_rows is not None:
        new_table = Table(names=colnames, rows=current_rows)
        new_table.meta["initial_comments"] = initial_comments.strip().split("\n")
        new_table.meta["comments"] = comment_text.strip().split("\n")
        table_list.append(new_table)

    return table_list
method_cache.astropy/io/ascii/core.py:DefaultSplitter:
class DefaultSplitter(BaseSplitter):
    """Default class to split strings into columns using python csv.  The class
    attributes are taken from the csv Dialect class.

    Typical usage::

      # lines = ..
      splitter = ascii.DefaultSplitter()
      for col_vals in splitter(lines):
          for col_val in col_vals:
               ...

    """

    delimiter = " "
    """ one-character string used to separate fields. """
    quotechar = '"'
    """ control how instances of *quotechar* in a field are quoted """
    doublequote = True
    """ character to remove special meaning from following character """
    escapechar = None
    """ one-character stringto quote fields containing special characters """
    quoting = csv.QUOTE_MINIMAL
    """ control when quotes are recognized by the reader """
    skipinitialspace = True
    """ ignore whitespace immediately following the delimiter """
    csv_writer = None
    csv_writer_out = StringIO()

    def process_line(self, line):
        """Remove whitespace at the beginning or end of line.  This is especially useful for
        whitespace-delimited files to prevent spurious columns at the beginning or end.
        If splitting on whitespace then replace unquoted tabs with space first.
        """
        if self.delimiter == r"\s":
            line = _replace_tab_with_space(line, self.escapechar, self.quotechar)
        return line.strip() + "\n"

    def process_val(self, val):
        """Remove whitespace at the beginning or end of value."""
        return val.strip(" \t")

    def __call__(self, lines):
        """Return an iterator over the table ``lines``, where each iterator output
        is a list of the split line values.

        Parameters
        ----------
        lines : list
            List of table lines

        Yields
        ------
        line : list of str
            Each line's split values.

        """
        if self.process_line:
            lines = [self.process_line(x) for x in lines]

        delimiter = " " if self.delimiter == r"\s" else self.delimiter

        csv_reader = csv.reader(
            lines,
            delimiter=delimiter,
            doublequote=self.doublequote,
            escapechar=self.escapechar,
            quotechar=self.quotechar,
            quoting=self.quoting,
            skipinitialspace=self.skipinitialspace,
        )
        for vals in csv_reader:
            if self.process_val:
                yield [self.process_val(x) for x in vals]
            else:
                yield vals

    def join(self, vals):
        delimiter = " " if self.delimiter is None else str(self.delimiter)

        if self.csv_writer is None:
            self.csv_writer = CsvWriter(
                delimiter=delimiter,
                doublequote=self.doublequote,
                escapechar=self.escapechar,
                quotechar=self.quotechar,
                quoting=self.quoting,
            )
        if self.process_val:
            vals = [self.process_val(x) for x in vals]
        out = self.csv_writer.writerow(vals).rstrip("\r\n")

        return out
method_cache.astropy/io/ascii/basic.py:CommentedHeade:
class CommentedHeaderHeader(BasicHeader):
    """
    Header class for which the column definition line starts with the
    comment character.  See the :class:`CommentedHeader` class  for an example.
    """

    def process_lines(self, lines):
        """
        Return only lines that start with the comment regexp.  For these
        lines strip out the matching characters.
        """
        re_comment = re.compile(self.comment)
        for line in lines:
            match = re_comment.match(line)
            if match:
                yield line[match.end() :]

    def write(self, lines):
        lines.append(self.write_comment + self.splitter.join(self.colnames))

The line is stripped and matched; if we uppercase it, the data lines with 'NO' also become 'NO' (still same), but numbers unaffected. However, grouping for data and new lines uses regex that relies on digits and 'NO' and 'nan' - these would be uppercased and 'nan' becomes 'NAN' which would break numeric detection. So better approach: make regex for commands case-insensitive only, by compiling with re.IGNORECASE or embedding (?i) in the command group. The regex _type_re composed includes command group; compiling with flags parameter would affect all groups, including 'nan' matching which should be case-insensitive? The data regex includes 'nan' in '[-+]?nan' — making regex case-insensitive would allow 'NaN', 'NAN' which is acceptable since 'nan' should be case-insensitive in files. Tool: get_file_context astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py
- Why: to read the QDP reader implementation and see how lines/commands are parsed.
- What I expected: the function that classifies lines and the code that interprets READ SERR / READ TERR commands.

Findings (summary of the code inspected)
1) _line_type(line, delimiter=None)
- Key lines:
  - _decimal_re = r"[+-]?(\d+(\.\d*)?|\.\d+)([eE][+-]?\d+)?"
  - _command_re = r"READ [TS]ERR(\s+[0-9]+)+"
  - _type_re composes command/new/data regex and compiles: _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re)
  - The code strips the line and uses _line_type_re.match(line). If match is None -> raise ValueError(f"Unrecognized QDP line: {line}").

- Problematic point:
  - _command_re is the literal uppercase "READ [TS]ERR..." and re.compile is used with no flags. That makes the command matching case-sensitive. So an input like "read serr 1 2" will not match the command branch and instead trigger the ValueError.

2) _get_tables_from_qdp_file(...)
- Key lines:
  - lines = _get_lines_from_file(...)
  - contents, ncol = _get_type_from_list_of_lines(lines, delimiter=delimiter)  # which uses _line_type
  - When encountering data lines, they parse the previously collected command_lines:
      for cline in command_lines.strip().split("\n"):
          command = cline.strip().split()
          if len(command) < 3:
              continue
          err_specs[command[1].lower()] = [int(c) for c in command[2:]]

- Observations:
  - The code *expects* command[1] to be 'SERR' or 'TERR' (they lower() it), so once the command line is recognized as a command, the rest of the pipeline handles mixed case (command[1].lower()) correctly.
  - The mismatch is only in detection: the regex in _line_type prevents lowercase 'read serr' from being recognized as a command.

3) Tests
- Tests for QDP (astropy/io/ascii/tests/test_qdp.py) use uppercase READ SERR / READ TERR. There were no tests for lowercase commands, explaining how this slipped through.

Diagnosis (root cause)
- The QDP reader's _line_type uses a regex that requires the command token to be in uppercase (r"READ [TS]ERR(...)"), and the regex is compiled without case-insensitivity. Therefore lowercase (or mixed case) command lines like "read serr 1 2" do not match and are treated as unrecognized, raising ValueError.
- Later code expects to be able to lowercase the second token (SERR/TERR), so the only missing piece is case-insensitive recognition of the command lines.

Minimal fix direction (specific code change)
- Change the compilation of the _type_re regex in _line_type to be case-insensitive. Concretely, in astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py, change:

    _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re)

  to:

    _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re, re.IGNORECASE)

Rationale:
- This makes the command matching accept "READ SERR", "read serr", "Read Serr", etc.
- It also allows variations in case for 'nan' and 'NO' in the regex matching, which is probably acceptable (and even desirable).
- It is a minimal, local change with low risk, because the rest of the code either lowercases command tokens (err_specs[command[1].lower()]) or handles numeric parsing unaffected by regex flags.

Where exactly to change
- The change should be applied in the _line_type(...) function, at the line that compiles the regex (currently line ~71 in the file shown earlier). Replace:

    _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re)

  with:

    _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re, re.IGNORECASE)

Secondary findings / related issues
- Data literal 'NO' detection is case-sensitive in _get_tables_from_qdp_file when parsing values:

    for v in line.split(delimiter):
        if v == "NO":
            values.append(np.ma.masked)
        else:
            ...

  That means a data line using lowercase 'no' would not be recognized as a masked value. The proposed change (making the regex compile re.IGNORECASE) only affects the line-type regex. It does not change the comparison v == "NO". If there are QDP files that use 'no' lowercase for missing values, those would still not be parsed as masked values. This may be a separate bug (and likely worth addressing), but the user-reported issue was about commands only.

- Regex case-insensitivity will also affect matching of 'nan' within the numeric-pattern. Accepting 'NaN'/'NAN' is generally desirable and unlikely to introduce regressions.

- I did not find other files using a similar command regex (search showed only this module). Tests currently assume uppercase commands; adding a test for mixed/lowercase commands is recommended.

Tests to add
- Add a unit test in astropy/io/ascii/tests/test_qdp.py to verify that lowercase commands are accepted, e.g.:

    example_qdp = "read serr 1 2\n1 0.5 1 0.5\n"
    t = Table.read(example_qdp, format='ascii.qdp', names=['col1', 'col2'])
    assert 'col1_err' in t.colnames  # or similar assertion to confirm errors parsed

- Optionally add tests for tolerant 'no' lowercase behavior, if desired: a test for 'no' vs 'NO' in data parsing.

Potential collateral impact / concerns
- Because re.IGNORECASE affects the entire _type_re, it makes the 'NO' and 'nan' matching in that regex case-insensitive. This is desirable.
- The code that tokenizes and processes commands already uses .lower() for the command token and thus is robust to mixed case.
- If some external code relied on _line_type raising for lowercase commands, this change would alter behavior; but that seems unlikely and undesirable anyway.
- If we want to also accept lowercase 'no' in the data parsing, we should modify the value parsing code to compare case-insensitively (e.g., if v.upper() == "NO") or normalize v before checks; that is a separate change because it affects actual data value parsing logic and has a higher chance of regression. Given the report only mentions commands, the minimal fix is to make command matching case-insensitive.

Summary / final recommendation
- Primary bug: _line_type uses a case-sensitive regex for the QDP commands (expects uppercase READ SERR / READ TERR). Lowercase commands such as "read serr 1 2" fail to match and trigger ValueError.
- Proposed minimal fix: compile the regex with re.IGNORECASE in _line_type:

    _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re, re.IGNORECASE)

- Tests: add a test with lowercase commands to ensure this behaviour is covered going forward.
- Secondary note: consider whether literal markers in data ('NO') should be compared case-insensitively when parsing values; if desired, this requires a small additional change in the value parsing (e.g., v.upper() == "NO").

    """
    Header class for which the column definition line starts with the
    comment character.  See the :class:`CommentedHeader` class  for an example.
    """

    def process_lines(self, lines):
        """
        Return only lines that start with the comment regexp.  For these
        lines strip out the matching characters.
        """
        re_comment = re.compile(self.comment)
        for line in lines:
            match = re_comment.match(line)
            if match:
                yield line[match.end() :]

    def write(self, lines):
        lines.append(self.write_comment + self.splitter.join(self.colnames))", "type": "class"}

Thought: Now I'll summarize the architecture map per the Steps. I have inspected the file, its components, parent classes, siblings, uses, and tests. I'll prepare the structured summary.
I will map all code related to the QDP reader so a subsequent change (fixing case-sensitivity of QDP commands) can be made safely. Below I document each tool call (why I made it), the results, what I learned from it, and what that implies for the scope of a fix.

Summary of the issue that motivated this investigation
- Symptom: Table.read(..., format='ascii.qdp') raises ValueError: "Unrecognized QDP line: read serr 1 2" when the command line uses lowercase "read serr ...".
- Likely cause: the QDP reader's line classification expects uppercase commands (case-sensitive regex), so lowercase commands don't match and are treated as invalid.

Tool calls, findings, and implications

1) Find the source of the ValueError message
- Tool: search_codebase for "Unrecognized QDP line"
- Why: to locate the file and function that raises the error.
- Result: located in astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py where the exception is raised after failing to match a compiled regex.
- What I learned: This confirms the QDP reader code lives in astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py and that the failure occurs in its line-classification logic. Fix scope is at least that file.

2) Read the full file astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py
- Tool: get_file_context astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py (entire file)
- Why: to enumerate every function, class, and global variable in the module, and to find all code paths interacting with QDP commands and data.
- What I learned (complete mapping of the file):
  - Functions:
    - _line_type(line, delimiter=None)
      - Uses a number regex, builds several regex parts:
        - _decimal_re
        - _command_re = r"READ [TS]ERR(\s+[0-9]+)+"
        - _new_re for NO lines
        - _data_re for numeric/data lines
        - _type_re combines them and compiles with re.compile(_type_re)
      - Strips the line and matches with _line_type_re.match(line). If no match, raises ValueError("Unrecognized QDP line: {line}")
      - Returns "comment", "command", "new", or "data,N" where N is number of columns.
      - Key observation: _command_re is uppercase and the regex is compiled with no flags -> case-sensitive matching.
    - _get_type_from_list_of_lines(lines, delimiter=None)
      - Calls _line_type on each line; enforces consistent number of data columns.
    - _get_lines_from_file(qdp_file)
      - Accepts several input representations (multiline string, file path, iterable).
    - _interpret_err_lines(err_specs, ncols, names=None)
      - Builds list of column names accounting for 'serr' and 'terr' error specifications.
    - _get_tables_from_qdp_file(qdp_file, input_colnames=None, delimiter=None)
      - Uses _get_lines_from_file and _get_type_from_list_of_lines.
      - Accumulates comments, command lines, and data lines.
      - When encountering first data lines and command_lines present, it tokenizes command_lines with .split() and builds err_specs as:
          err_specs[command[1].lower()] = [int(c) for c in command[2:]]
        i.e., it lowercases command[1] (so it expects SERR/TERR) and canonicalizes to 'serr'/'terr'.
      - When parsing data values, it treats a value exactly equal to "NO" as masked (v == "NO"); numeric parsing attempts int then float otherwise.
    - _read_table_qdp(qdp_file, names=None, table_id=None, delimiter=None)
      - Calls _get_tables_from_qdp_file and returns the appropriate table_id.
    - _write_table_qdp(table, filename=None, err_specs=None)
      - Writes READ SERR and READ TERR commands in uppercase (print(f"READ SERR {col_string}", file=fobj)).
      - Writes NO as the representation for masked values.
  - Classes:
    - QDPSplitter(core.DefaultSplitter)
      - delimiter = " "
      - Inherits splitting behavior from core.DefaultSplitter.
    - QDPHeader(basic.CommentedHeaderHeader)
      - splitter_class = QDPSplitter
      - comment = "!"
      - write_comment = "!"
    - QDPData(basic.BasicData)
      - splitter_class = QDPSplitter
      - fill_values = [(core.masked, "NO")]
      - comment = "!"
      - write_comment = None
    - QDP(basic.Basic)
      - format metadata (_format_name = "qdp", ...), sets header_class and data_class to above.
      - __init__ stores table_id, names, err_specs, delimiter
      - read() uses self.inputter.get_lines to obtain lines then delegates to _read_table_qdp
      - write() delegates to _write_table_qdp
  - Tests in the module call the helper functions and classes (see tests elsewhere).
- Implication: The likely minimal change is in _line_type where the regex is compiled. Because downstream code explicitly lowercases the command token when building err_specs, only detection (matching) is case-sensitive now and preventing lowercase commands from being processed.

3) Inspect core and basic base classes used by QDP
- Tools:
  - search_codebase/class lookup and get_code for core.DefaultSplitter, basic.CommentedHeaderHeader, basic.BasicData, basic.Basic.
- Why: to see inherited behavior that can affect splitting, line processing, or how the QDP reader is used by the table read/write machinery.
- What I learned:
  - core.DefaultSplitter is in astropy/io/ascii/core.py and sets delimiter = " " and defines process_line/process_val splitting behavior using csv.reader; no dependence on QDP command parsing.
  - basic.CommentedHeaderHeader strips comment characters for header lines; QDPHeader inherits this and sets comment/write_comment to "!".
  - basic.BasicData sets default comment regex r"\s*#" (QDPData overrides comment to "!").
  - basic.Basic combines header_class and data_class into a reader; QDP inherits from basic.Basic so reading/writing logic (how the components combine) is standard.
- Implication:
  - No other inherited behavior will be negatively affected by a local change to regex compilation in _line_type. Splitting and value parsing are delegated to the Basic/DefaultSplitter code; we must still ensure delimiter handling remains consistent.

4) Inspect tests for qdp behavior and whether mixed case is covered
- Tool: get_file_context astropy/io/ascii/tests/test_qdp.py
- Why: to see what is currently tested and whether lowercase commands are covered (they are not).
- What I learned:
  - All existing tests use uppercase READ SERR / READ TERR. No test covers lowercase commands.
  - Tests exercise NO, nan, etc., and roundtrip behavior; they also exercise writing READ SERR/TERR (writer prints them uppercase).
- Implication:
  - A new test is needed to prevent regressions: add one that uses lowercase 'read serr' (and possibly mixed case) to ensure commands are accepted. Current tests don't guard against this bug.

5) Search for other occurrences of READ SERR or the command regex
- Tool: search_codebase for 'READ SERR' and for comparisons to "NO"
- Why: to detect other modules or code paths that hardcode command literals case-sensitively or depend on uppercase assumptions.
- What I learned:
  - The literal 'READ SERR' appears in qdp.py docstrings, tests, and in the writer which prints uppercase commands when writing.
  - The only code comparing to "NO" is in qdp._get_tables_from_qdp_file where v == "NO".
- Implication:
  - The only code that needs changing is inside qdp.py. No other modules appear to be matching QDP commands case-sensitively. However, note that data-level literal 'NO' comparison is case-sensitive — this could be an additional edge case if input files use lowercase 'no'.

6) Trace callers and consumers of the primary functions
- Tools: get_subgraph was available; I used search and file reading to find where functions/classes are called.
- Why: to map who calls _line_type/_get_tables_from_qdp_file and how the QDP reader is used.
- What I learned:
  - _read_table_qdp is used by QDP.read (which is registered as ascii.qdp format). Table.read ultimately calls the registered reader. _get_tables_from_qdp_file is used only in this module, via _read_table_qdp.
- Implication:
  - The change will only directly affect the QDP reader; other readers are unaffected.

7) Note of possible adjacent issues discovered
- Data 'NO' is tested by exact equality v == "NO", so lowercase 'no' in a data value would not be treated as masked. The regex in _type_re contains 'NO' as part of matching new-table lines and data values. If we make the regex case-insensitive (see minimal fix below), it will also allow "no" or "NaN" variations at the matching level, but the later explicit check v == "NO" will still be case-sensitive.

Complete architecture map (structured)

    - _get_type_from_list_of_lines(lines, delimiter=None):
      - Calls _line_type for each line; ensures consistent column count across the file.
    - _get_lines_from_file(qdp_file):
      - Input acceptance: file path, text containing newlines, or iterable of lines.
    - _interpret_err_lines(err_specs, ncols, names=None):
      - Builds column names including error columns based on serr/terr specs, expects err_specs keys are 'serr' and 'terr'.
    - _get_tables_from_qdp_file(qdp_file, input_colnames=None, delimiter=None):
      - Parses file contents into tables: collects command lines (text) and then, upon encountering data lines, tokenizes command lines via .split() and builds err_specs as err_specs[command[1].lower()] = ...

    - _read_table_qdp(qdp_file, ...):
      - Entrypoint used by QDP.read; returns a specific table by table_id.
    - _write_table_qdp(table, filename=None, err_specs=None):
      - Writes READ SERR/TERR commands in uppercase.
      - Represents masked values as "NO".

      - delimiter = " "

    - QDPHeader(basic.CommentedHeaderHeader):
      - splitter_class = QDPSplitter
      - comment/write_comment = "!"
    - QDPData(basic.BasicData):
      - splitter_class = QDPSplitter

Implications for the proposed fix direction (NOT a fix, just the impact map)
- Root cause confirmed: _line_type uses a case-sensitive regex for commands (expects uppercase "READ [TS]ERR...") and compiles with no re.IGNORECASE flag; hence "read serr 1 2" is not recognized as a command and ends up failing.
- Minimal area to change (single spot) in terms of recognizing lowercase READ commands:
  - _line_type: change the regex compilation to use case-insensitive matching. For example, compile with re.IGNORECASE.
  - That will allow "READ SERR", "read serr", "Read Serr", etc. to be recognized as command lines.
- Collateral consequences to examine before actually changing code:
  - Making the regex case-insensitive will affect the entire _type_re: it will also make 'NO' and 'nan' token matching case-insensitive at the regex level. That seems desirable for 'nan' (NaN, NAN), and acceptable for 'NO' (NO/no), but note: _get_tables_from_qdp_file later uses exact string equality v == "NO" to convert to masked. So even if the regex recognizes a line as "new" because of a lowercase "no no no", the downstream logic will not consider tokens equal to "NO" if the file used lowercase 'no'. So there is an inconsistency:
    - Regex match becomes case-insensitive -> command and 'new' lines that use lowercase will be recognized correctly.
    - But the token-level parsing uses v == "NO" (case-sensitive), so lowercase "no" tokens will not be converted to masked values by the explicit check.
  - Impact summary: making the regex case-insensitive will solve the immediate bug (lowercase READ/ERR commands), but if some QDP files also use lowercase 'no' to indicate masked values then those data cells will not be masked properly because of the exact string check in _get_tables_from_qdp_file.
  - The writer always emits "NO" uppercase, so write/read roundtrip would stay consistent as long as reading uses uppercase "NO". But if the reader is made tolerant of lowercase "read serr", it may be worth making data-level "NO" detection case-insensitive too (change to if v.strip().upper() == "NO" or similar).
- Other places in qdp.py to consider:
  - _get_tables_from_qdp_file: masked-value detection (v == "NO") — candidate for normalization if the project intends to accept lowercase "no".
  - Tests: there are no tests covering lowercase commands or lowercase "no". New tests should be added to cover:
    - Lowercase commands "read serr 1 2" are accepted and parsed into err_specs.
    - Lowercase "no" as a masked value (if read tolerance is extended) — decide whether that is desired.
  - Docstrings: several docstrings and examples in qdp.py show uppercase commands; if the project wants to document case-insensitivity, add a doctest or example with lowercase commands.

- Primary location that must be examined/changed:
  - astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py::_line_type (line where regex is compiled):
    - currently: _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re)
    - effects: compile flags change affects entire pattern
- Secondary locations that might need follow-up changes or tests:

    - line parsing of commands: err_specs[command[1].lower()] = ...
      - This already lowercases the command token, so case-insensitive recognition will integrate smoothly here.
    - masked-value detection: for v in line.split(delimiter): if v == "NO": ...  — consider making this case-insensitive if project intends to accept lowercase "no".
  - astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py::_write_table_qdp
    - Emits uppercase "READ SERR" / "READ TERR" and "NO". No change required for reading-leniency but note writer output will continue to be uppercase.
  - Tests:
    - astropy/io/ascii/tests/test_qdp.py — no test uses lowercase commands. Add a test that reads a simple file like:
      - "read serr 1 2\n1 0.5 1 0.5\n"  and assert columns and error columns are parsed.
    - Possibly add a test for "no" lowercase masked data if adopting that behavior.

Suggested tests to add (for the next phase)
- A small test in test_qdp.py to verify lowercase command parsing:
  - Input: "read serr 1 2\n1 0.5 1 0.5\n"
  - Call _read_table_qdp or Table.read(..., format="ascii.qdp") and assert returned table has the expected columns and that error columns are present and correctly parsed.

Open questions and gaps that should be rechecked by the fix implementer
- Decide desired semantics for data-level "NO" token case-sensitivity:
  - Should lowercase "no" be interpreted as masked and/or should it be accepted in the separator line (line of NOs)? If yes, update _get_tables_from_qdp_file token check (v == "NO") to a normalized comparison (e.g., v.strip().upper() == "NO").

Concise conclusion / map for the person who will implement the fix
- The failure "Unrecognized QDP line: read serr 1 2" is caused by the command regex in _line_type being case-sensitive (it includes literal uppercase "READ [TS]ERR" and the pattern is compiled with no flags).
