Goal recap
- You asked me to map every piece of code that could be affected by the previously-identified fix direction (make QDP command recognition case-insensitive). I collected all source in the QDP reader module and all related components so the next engineer can precisely evaluate and implement a safe fix.
- I did not implement a fix. I collected code, traced call sites, and documented all places that would be touched (directly or indirectly) by making command recognition case-insensitive.

Before each operation I explain why I looked where I did, and after each tool result I summarize what I learned and what that implies for scope of a future fix.

1) Locate QDP reader code (why)
- I searched for the ValueError text from the bug report ("Unrecognized QDP line") to find the QDP reader implementation.

What I ran
- Searched the repo for the error message and opened astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py.

What I learned (summary)
- The QDP reader implementation is in astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py (commit id shown in tool output). The key functions and classes in that file are responsible for:
  - classifying lines (_line_type)
  - splitting/identifying blocks of lines (_get_type_from_list_of_lines)
  - assembling tables from the classified lines (_get_tables_from_qdp_file / _read_table_qdp)
  - writing QDP format (_write_table_qdp)
  - helper functions: _get_lines_from_file, _interpret_err_lines, _understand_err_col
  - classes: QDPSplitter, QDPHeader, QDPData, QDP (the reader/writer class exported to the io system)

Implication
- The bug reported (lowercase "read serr" not recognized) points directly at _line_type where command detection is implemented with an apparently case-sensitive regex.

2) Read the whole QDP module (why)
- To map every function and class in the module so we know all code that could be affected by making command matching case-insensitive.

What I fetched
- Entire file astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py (all 642 lines).

Key points and exact locations (from file contents)
- _line_type(line, delimiter=None) — lines 20–86:
  - Builds regex parts:
    - _decimal_re (numeric)
    - _command_re = r"READ [TS]ERR(\s+[0-9]+)+"
    - _new_re = rf"NO({sep}NO)+"
    - _data_re = rf"({_decimal_re}|NO|[-+]?nan)({sep}({_decimal_re}|NO|[-+]?nan))*)"
    - _type_re built from the above and compiled:
      _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re)
  - Classifies lines: comment, command, data (returning "data,N" for N columns), new (full of NOs).
  - If no match: raise ValueError(f"Unrecognized QDP line: {line}")
  - -> Important: _command_re uses literal uppercase 'READ' and 'SERR/TERR'; regex is compiled without flags, hence case-sensitive.

- _get_type_from_list_of_lines(lines, delimiter=None) — lines 88–132:
  - Calls _line_type for each line to build types list and checks consistent ncol for data lines.

- _get_lines_from_file(qdp_file) — lines 135–146:
  - Accepts a filename, a long string with newlines, or a list of strings.

- _interpret_err_lines(err_specs, ncols, names=None) — lines 149–234:
  - Builds column names based on err_specs (expected dict like {'serr': [n...], 'terr': [n...]}) and number of columns.
  - Uses serr_cols = err_specs.pop("serr", []) and terr_cols = err_specs.pop("terr", [])

- _get_tables_from_qdp_file(qdp_file, input_colnames=None, delimiter=None) — lines 237–342:
  - Core read logic that iterates lines and their types:
    - collects comments and command_lines
    - when encountering first data after commands, it parses command_lines:
      for cline in command_lines.strip().split("\n"):
          command = cline.strip().split()
          if len(command) < 3: continue
          err_specs[command[1].lower()] = [int(c) for c in command[2:]]
      (so the code lowercases command[1] at use time)
    - constructs colnames via _interpret_err_lines(err_specs, ncol, names=input_colnames)
    - data parsing: for v in line.split(delimiter):
        if v == "NO":
            values.append(np.ma.masked)
        else:
            try int -> else float
  - "new" line handling and creation of Table objects, storing initial/comments in meta.

- _understand_err_col(colnames) — lines 345–383:
  - Used by writing/reading to determine which columns are errors and how to label them. Works with suffixes "_err", "_perr", "_nerr".

- _read_table_qdp(qdp_file, names=None, table_id=None, delimiter=None) — lines 385–423:
  - Wraps _get_tables_from_qdp_file, issues warning if table_id None.

- _write_table_qdp(table, filename=None, err_specs=None) — lines 425–492:
  - Writes initial comments, then prints READ SERR and READ TERR lines (uppercase), prints comments and header (prefixed with '!') and data rows.
  - For masked values it writes "NO" uppercase:
      if not np.ma.is_masked(val):
          rep = str(val)
      else:
          rep = "NO"
  - Returns the output as list of lines (strings).

- Class QDPSplitter(core.DefaultSplitter) — lines 494–501:
  - delimiter = " " (space) — used by QDPHeader/QDPData splitters.

- Class QDPHeader(basic.CommentedHeaderHeader) — lines 502–510:
  - splitter_class = QDPSplitter
  - comment = "!"
  - write_comment = "!"

- Class QDPData(basic.BasicData) — lines 512–521:
  - splitter_class = QDPSplitter
  - fill_values = [(core.masked, "NO")]  (class-level)
  - comment = "!"
  - write_comment = None

- Class QDP(basic.Basic) — lines 523–642:
  - class-level attributes:
    - _format_name = "qdp"
    - _io_registry_can_write = True
    - _io_registry_suffix = ".qdp"
    - header_class = QDPHeader
    - data_class = QDPData
  - instance attributes set in __init__: table_id, names, err_specs, delimiter (sep)
  - read(self, table) → self.lines = self.inputter.get_lines(...); then calls _read_table_qdp(self.lines, table_id=self.table_id, names=..., delimiter=...)
  - write(self, table) → calls _write_table_qdp and returns lines; calls self._check_multidim_table first.

What this implies about the fix's scope
- The recognition of QDP commands is implemented entirely in _line_type via a regex. That is the single point where the command is recognized as a "command" vs data/new/comment.
- Once a command line is recognized, the code that interprets the command lowercases the token for indexing into err_specs (err_specs[command[1].lower()]) — i.e., downstream logic already expects mixed case and will accept serr/terr in any case once command lines are recognized.
- The main problem is detection: 'read serr 1 2' does not match the uppercase literal in _command_re and so _line_type fails to match, raising the ValueError observed.

3) Inspect component/associated classes (why)
- Changing command recognition could affect how header/data splitters and fill_values behave. QDP module sets QDPData.fill_values and QDPHeader.splitter_class. I fetched basic/core to understand how fill_values and splitters are used.

What I fetched
- astropy/io/ascii/core.py (relevant parts) — especially BaseHeader/BaseData behaviors and fill_values handling.
- astropy/io/ascii/basic.py for CommentedHeaderHeader/Basic etc. (we already saw QDPHeader/QDPData inherit from these).

What I learned
- core.BaseData/Reader machinery has a fill_values mechanism:
  - BaseData.fill_values is interpreted in _set_fill_values and used in _set_masks/_replace_vals.
  - The mechanism checks membership using exact equality (e.g., if x in col.fill_values) — i.e., case-sensitive string comparisons.
- QDPData defines fill_values = [(core.masked, "NO")] (class-level). The core machinery will map this to column-level col.fill_values['NO'] = '...' and then check exact string membership while replacing/masking. That means the code in the core path is case-sensitive for literal replacements.
- However, in the QDP reader code the module does its own manual parsing of data lines (for v in line.split(delimiter): if v == "NO": values.append(np.ma.masked) ...). This bypasses the generic fill_values replacement (though QDPData.fill_values is still defined).
- The writer always emits 'READ SERR' and 'NO' uppercase.

Implications for fix
- If you make command detection case-insensitive at the regex level, you must examine whether data values like 'NO' or 'nan' should be accepted in lower or mixed case. Two places will be impacted:
  - The regex in _line_type: if compiled with a case-insensitive flag, it will also make the data/new/nan matches in the regex case-insensitive (i.e., 'no' and 'NaN' would be accepted).
  - The manual data parsing code in _get_tables_from_qdp_file performs v == "NO" checks that remain case-sensitive; so e.g., after making regex matching case-insensitive, a line with lowercase 'no' (if present) may be classified as data/new but the value-level check would not treat 'no' as masked. That is a separate mismatch to be addressed if you want consistent case-insensitive behavior.

4) Inspect class hierarchy & siblings (why)
- To know what QDP inherits and whether siblings handle similar features (this reveals the canonical pattern).

What I fetched / searched
- basic.Basic (parent), core.BaseHeader/core.BaseData (parents of QDPHeader/QDPData).
- Searched for other formats that parse inline commands — none similar; the QDP module uniquely parses READ commands and NO separators.

What I learned
- QDP inherits from basic.Basic and only overrides header_class/data_class and provides its own small __init__, read, write wrappers. The heavy lifting for QDP parsing is module-level functions, not class instance methods.
- No sibling readers (Csv, Tab, Rdb, etc.) do the same command-line parsing. So this fix is localized to QDP module, but effects can touch core fill_values behavior and tests.

5) Trace all operations touching same data (why)
- The bug relates to command lines that indicate how many error columns follow certain data columns; so commands impact column naming (which in turn impacts table creation). We must list all code paths that touch these same data so a fix won't leave inconsistent assumptions.

What I traced
- Line classification: _line_type → identifies command lines (currently uppercase-only)
- Command collection: command_lines string in _get_tables_from_qdp_file
- Command parsing: command = cline.strip().split(); err_specs[command[1].lower()] = [int(c) for c in command[2:]] (so serr/terr keys lowercased)
- Column naming: _interpret_err_lines uses serr_cols/terr_cols to construct columns and error column names (_err, _perr, _nerr)
- Data parsing: in _get_tables_from_qdp_file, each data value v is processed:
  - if v == "NO": append masked
  - else try int, else float
- New table separation: _line_type recognizes full- 'NO' lines (pattern _new_re = rf"NO({sep}NO)+")

What this implies
- The command lines must be detected before data rows are parsed. The downstream parsing expects err_specs keys 'serr' and 'terr' (lowercase) and will work with mixed-case commands if they are recognized.
- The data parsing code checks v == "NO" exactly; that remains case-sensitive and would need normalization to support 'no' if you want to accept lowercase 'no' on read.

6) Callers and consumers (why)
- Need to see how the QDP class is used by the io system and tests; changing behavior can affect registry and tests.

What I checked
- core.MetaBaseReader registers _format_name = "qdp" class in FORMAT_CLASSES and the io registry (connect.io_registry.register_reader), so Table.read(... format='ascii.qdp') dispatches into QDP reader; nothing else calls the module-level helpers directly (except tests).
- The tests for qdp are in astropy/io/ascii/tests/test_qdp.py and call _read_table_qdp, _write_table_qdp directly as well as Table.read(... format='ascii.qdp').

What I learned about tests and expected behavior
- Current tests exercise uppercase command lines and uppercase 'NO' in data. There are no tests for lowercase commands or lowercase 'no' tokens. That is why the issue was not covered by test suite.

7) Hardcoded values and assumptions (where they are)
- In qdp.py:
  - _command_re uses literal uppercase "READ" and "SERR/TERR" (line ~63)
  - _new_re uses "NO" uppercase (line ~68)
  - _data_re contains "NO" and "nan" literal strings (line ~69)
  - _line_type uses re.compile(_type_re) with no flags (line ~71)
  - Data parsing in _get_tables_from_qdp_file uses v == "NO" exact match (line ~309)
  - _write_table_qdp emits "READ SERR"/"READ TERR" uppercase and writes masked values as "NO" uppercase (lines ~459–463, ~480)
- In QDPData:
  - fill_values = [(core.masked, "NO")] (class-level). That ties the masked literal string to uppercase "NO".

8) Concrete list of source locations that would be touched by a change to accept lowercase commands (so the fix implementer can review all impact)
- astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py:
  - _line_type:
    - _command_re = r"READ [TS]ERR(\s+[0-9]+)+"
    - _type_re assembled from command/new/data regex
    - _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re)  <-- change point (if making regex case-insensitive)
  - _get_tables_from_qdp_file:
    - When parsing the collected command_lines (err_specs mapping): err_specs[command[1].lower()] = [int(c) for c in command[2:]]  (this already lowercases the token)
    - Data value parsing:
        for v in line.split(delimiter):
            if v == "NO":
                values.append(np.ma.masked)
            else:
                try: int(v)
                except: float(v)
      (v == "NO" is case-sensitive; potential secondary issue)
  - _write_table_qdp:
    - Emitted command strings are uppercase: print(f"READ SERR {col_string}", file=fobj); print(f"READ TERR {col_string}", file=fobj)
    - Writes masked values as "NO" uppercase.
  - QDPData.fill_values = [(core.masked, "NO")] — used by the core machinery in other contexts (but in QDP reading QDP module does its own v == "NO" parsing).

- Tests:
  - astropy/io/ascii/tests/test_qdp.py — add tests covering lowercase commands and (optionally) lowercase 'no' masked values.

9) Potential side-effects and follow-up items to examine (for the fix author to consider)
- If you make the line-type regex case-insensitive (e.g., compile with re.IGNORECASE or use (?i) for the command part), the change affects the entire _type_re. That would also make the 'NO' and 'nan' token checks in the regex accept mixed case (e.g., 'no' and 'NaN'). This is likely benign and desirable for 'nan', but it introduces a mismatch:
  - The regex matching will accept a "NO NO NO" line even if it is lowercase 'no no no', so lines will be classified as "new" or "data" accordingly.
  - Downstream manual parsing still uses v == "NO" (case-sensitive), so lowercase 'no' in data lines will not be masked by this check; the line classification might accept the line as data/new, but the masked token won't be treated as masked — inconsistent behavior.
- Two approaches to keep behavior consistent:
  - (A) Make only the command subpattern case-insensitive (e.g., inline (?i:...) for the command group) so that data tokens remain matched case-sensitively; or
  - (B) If making the entire regex case-insensitive, also update value parsing to normalize tokens (e.g., v.strip().upper() == "NO") so masked values are correctly recognized in any case.
- The existing downstream code already lowercases the second token of command lines when building err_specs (err_specs[command[1].lower()]). So once commands are recognized, the rest of the pipeline already tolerates mixed-case 'SERR'/'serr'.

10) Tests to add (recommended)
- Add a unit test in astropy/io/ascii/tests/test_qdp.py to assert that lowercase/mixed-case command lines are accepted:
  - minimal example:
    example_qdp = "read serr 1 2\n1 0.5 1 0.5\n"

bug_report.Title: ascii.qdp: QDP reader should accept case-insensitive commands (e.g. 'read serr')
bug_report.Description: The QDP ASCII reader (astropy.io.ascii.qdp) treats QDP command lines (for example "READ SERR 1 2") as case-sensitive. The line-classification regex in _line_type only matches uppercase commands, so lowercase or mixed-case commands such as "read serr 1 2" are not recognized and raise ValueError: "Unrecognized QDP line: read serr 1 2". The downstream code expects to be able to lowercase tokens (e.g. command[1].lower()), so the core issue is only the case-sensitive detection of command lines. Additionally, data token detection for masked values uses an exact, case-sensitive comparison (if v == "NO"), which would miss lowercase 'no' tokens if present.
bug_report.RootCause: The regex used to classify QDP lines in _line_type is compiled without the re.IGNORECASE flag, while _command_re contains literal uppercase tokens (e.g. 'READ [TS]ERR(...)'). As a result, command lines in lowercase or mixed case fail to match the 'command' branch and are treated as unrecognized lines. Separately, value-level masked-token detection in _get_tables_from_qdp_file compares tokens to the uppercase string "NO" exactly, so lowercase 'no' tokens are not converted to masked values.
StepsToReproduce: Create a file test.qdp with contents:
Observe ValueError: Unrecognized QDP line: read serr 1 2
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Table.read(..., format='ascii.qdp') should accept QDP command lines regardless of case (e.g. 'READ SERR', 'read serr', 'Read Serr') and parse the error columns. Optionally, data tokens representing masked values (e.g. 'NO') should be recognized case-insensitively if the project policy prefers that.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Lowercase or mixed-case QDP command lines (e.g. 'read serr 1 2') are not recognized by the reader and cause a ValueError: 'Unrecognized QDP line: read serr 1 2'.
bug_report.Suggestions: 1) Minimal, local fix: compile the combined line-type regex in _line_type with re.IGNORECASE so command lines are matched case-insensitively. 2) Address the data-token masked-value gap by normalizing tokens in _get_tables_from_qdp_file before comparing to 'NO' (e.g., v_stripped.upper() == 'NO'). This keeps the scope local to the QDP reader and avoids broader changes in core._set_fill_values. 3) Add unit tests in astropy/io/ascii/tests/test_qdp.py that include lowercase and mixed-case command lines (e.g., 'read serr 1 2') and optionally lowercase 'no' tokens to prevent regressions. 4) Update docstrings/doctests in astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py to include examples with lowercase/mixed-case commands. 5) Consider (optional, larger-scope) whether core._set_fill_values should perform case-insensitive fill-value matching project-wide; if so, update core and readers and add tests accordingly. Recommended immediate changes are (1) and (2) above.
bug_report.possible_fix: Make the line-type regex case-insensitive by compiling with re.IGNORECASE in _line_type. Normalize parsed data tokens in _get_tables_from_qdp_file (strip and compare uppercase) so 'no'/'NO' are both treated as masked values. Add a unit test in astropy/io/ascii/tests/test_qdp.py that contains a lowercase/mixed-case command and asserts the table (including error columns) is parsed. Update docstrings/doctests to demonstrate that commands are case-insensitive.

possible_fix_code.astropy.io.ascii.qdp._line_type:
def _line_type(line, delimiter=None):
    """Interpret a QDP file line.

    Parameters
    ----------
    line : str
        a single line of the file

    Returns
    -------
    type : str
        Line type: "comment", "command", or "data"

    Examples
    --------
    >>> _line_type("READ SERR 3")
    'command'
    >>> _line_type(" \\\n    ...     !some gibberish")
    'comment'
    >>> _line_type("   ")
    'comment'
    >>> _line_type(" 21345.45")
    'data,1'
    >>> _line_type(" 21345.45 1.53e-3 1e-3 .04 NO nan")
    'data,6'
    >>> _line_type(" 21345.45,1.53e-3,1e-3,.04,NO,nan", delimiter=',')
    'data,6'
    >>> _line_type(" 21345.45 ! a comment to disturb")
    'data,1'
    >>> _line_type("NO NO NO NO NO")
    'new'
    >>> _line_type("NO,NO,NO,NO,NO", delimiter=',')
    'new'
    >>> _line_type("N O N NOON OON O")
    Traceback (most recent call last):
        ...
    ValueError: Unrecognized QDP line...
    >>> _line_type(" some non-comment gibberish")
    Traceback (most recent call last):
        ...
    ValueError: Unrecognized QDP line...
    """
    _decimal_re = r"[+-]?(\d+(\.\d*)?|\.\d+)([eE][+-]?\d+)?"
    _command_re = r"READ [TS]ERR(\s+[0-9]+)+"

    sep = delimiter
    if delimiter is None:
        sep = r"\s+"
    _new_re = rf"NO({sep}NO)+"
    _data_re = rf"({_decimal_re}|NO|[-+]?nan)({sep}({_decimal_re}|NO|[-+]?nan))*)"
    _type_re = rf"^\s*((?P<command>{_command_re})|(?P<new>{_new_re})|(?P<data>{_data_re})?\s*(\!(?P<comment>.*))?\s*$"
    # Compile the line-type regex case-insensitively so commands like
    # 'read serr' or 'Read Serr' are accepted in addition to 'READ SERR'.
    _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re, re.IGNORECASE)
    line = line.strip()
    if not line:
        return "comment"
    match = _line_type_re.match(line)

    if match is None:
        raise ValueError(f"Unrecognized QDP line: {line}")
    for type_, val in match.groupdict().items():
        if val is None:
            continue
        if type_ == "data":
            return f"data,{len(val.split(sep=delimiter))}"
        else:
            return type_
possible_fix_code.astropy.io.ascii.qdp._get_tables_from_qd:
def _get_tables_from_qdp_file(qdp_file, input_colnames=None, delimiter=None):
    """Get all tables from a QDP file.

    Parameters
    ----------
    qdp_file : str
        Input QDP file name

    Other Parameters
    ----------------
    input_colnames : list of str
        Name of data columns (defaults to ['col1','col2', ...]), _not_
        including error columns.
    delimiter : str
        Delimiter for the values in the table.

    Returns
    -------
    list of `~astropy.table.Table`
        List containing all the tables present inside the QDP file
    """
    lines = _get_lines_from_file(qdp_file)
    contents, ncol = _get_type_from_list_of_lines(lines, delimiter=delimiter)

    table_list = []
    err_specs = {}
    colnames = None

    comment_text = ""
    initial_comments = ""
    command_lines = ""
    current_rows = None

    for line, datatype in zip(lines, contents):
        line = line.strip().lstrip("!")
        # Is this a comment?
        if datatype == "comment":
            comment_text += line + "\n"
            continue

        if datatype == "command":
            # The first time I find commands, I save whatever comments into
            # The initial comments.
            if command_lines == "":
                initial_comments = comment_text
                comment_text = ""

            if err_specs != {}:
                warnings.warn(
                    "This file contains multiple command blocks. Please verify",
                    AstropyUserWarning,
                )
            command_lines += line + "\n"
            continue

        if datatype.startswith("data"):
            # The first time I find data, I define err_specs
            if err_specs == {} and command_lines != "":
                for cline in command_lines.strip().split("\n"):
                    command = cline.strip().split()
                    # This should never happen, but just in case.
                    if len(command) < 3:
                        continue
                    err_specs[command[1].lower()] = [int(c) for c in command[2:]]
            if colnames is None:
                colnames = _interpret_err_lines(err_specs, ncol, names=input_colnames)

            if current_rows is None:
                current_rows = []

            values = []
            # Normalize each token before checking for masked-values so that
            # 'NO', 'no', or 'No' are treated consistently as masked.
            for v in line.split(delimiter):
                v_stripped = v.strip()
                if v_stripped.upper() == "NO":
                    values.append(np.ma.masked)
                else:
                    # Understand if number is int or float using the stripped token
                    try:
                        values.append(int(v_stripped))
                    except ValueError:
                        values.append(float(v_stripped))
            current_rows.append(values)
            continue

        if datatype == "new":
            # Save table to table_list and reset
            if current_rows is not None:
                new_table = Table(names=colnames, rows=current_rows)
                new_table.meta["initial_comments"] = initial_comments.strip().split(
                    "\n"
                )
                new_table.meta["comments"] = comment_text.strip().split("\n")
                # Reset comments
                comment_text = ""
                table_list.append(new_table)
                current_rows = None
            continue

    # At the very end, if there is still a table being written, let's save
    # it to the table_list
    if current_rows is not None:
        new_table = Table(names=colnames, rows=current_rows)
        new_table.meta["initial_comments"] = initial_comments.strip().split("\n")
        new_table.meta["comments"] = comment_text.strip().split("\n")
        table_list.append(new_table)

    return table_list

class NDDataRef(NDArithmeticMixin, NDIOMixin, NDSlicingMixin, NDData):
    """Implements `NDData` with all Mixins.

    This class implements a `NDData`-like container that supports reading and
    writing as implemented in the ``astropy.io.registry`` and also slicing
    (indexing) and simple arithmetic (add, subtract, divide and multiply).

    Notes
    -----
    A key distinction from `NDDataArray` is that this class does not attempt
    to provide anything that was not defined in any of the parent classes.

    See Also
    --------
    NDData
    NDArithmeticMixin
    NDSlicingMixin
    NDIOMixin

    Examples
    --------
    The mixins allow operation that are not possible with `NDData` or
    `NDDataBase`, i.e. simple arithmetic::

        >>> from astropy.nddata import NDDataRef, StdDevUncertainty
        >>> import numpy as np

        >>> data = np.ones((3,3), dtype=float)
        >>> ndd1 = NDDataRef(data, uncertainty=StdDevUncertainty(data))
        >>> ndd2 = NDDataRef(data, uncertainty=StdDevUncertainty(data))

        >>> ndd3 = ndd1.add(ndd2)
        >>> ndd3.data  # doctest: +FLOAT_CMP
        array([[2., 2., 2.],
               [2., 2., 2.],
               [2., 2., 2.]])
        >>> ndd3.uncertainty.array  # doctest: +FLOAT_CMP
        array([[1.41421356, 1.41421356, 1.41421356],
               [1.41421356, 1.41421356, 1.41421356],
               [1.41421356, 1.41421356, 1.41421356]])

    see `NDArithmeticMixin` for a complete list of all supported arithmetic
    operations.

    But also slicing (indexing) is possible::

        >>> ndd4 = ndd3[1,:]
        >>> ndd4.data  # doctest: +FLOAT_CMP
        array([2., 2., 2.])
        >>> ndd4.uncertainty.array  # doctest: +FLOAT_CMP
        array([1.41421356, 1.41421356, 1.41421356])

    See `NDSlicingMixin` for a description how slicing works (which attributes)
    are sliced.
    """

    pass

class NDArithmeticMixin:
    """
    Mixin class to add arithmetic to an NDData object.

    When subclassing, be sure to list the superclasses in the correct order
    so that the subclass sees NDData as the main superclass. See
    `~astropy.nddata.NDDataArray` for an example.

    Notes
    -----
    This class only aims at covering the most common cases so there are certain
    restrictions on the saved attributes::

        - ``uncertainty`` : has to be something that has a `NDUncertainty`-like
          interface for uncertainty propagation
        - ``mask`` : has to be something that can be used by a bitwise ``or``
          operation.
        - ``wcs`` : has to implement a way of comparing with ``=`` to allow
          the operation.

    But there is a workaround that allows to disable handling a specific
    attribute and to simply set the results attribute to ``None`` or to
    copy the existing attribute (and neglecting the other).
    For example for uncertainties not representing an `NDUncertainty`-like
    interface you can alter the ``propagate_uncertainties`` parameter in
    :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin.add`. ``None`` means that the result will have no
    uncertainty, ``False`` means it takes the uncertainty of the first operand
    (if this does not exist from the second operand) as the result's
    uncertainty. This behavior is also explained in the docstring for the
    different arithmetic operations.

    Decomposing the units is not attempted, mainly due to the internal mechanics
    of `~astropy.units.Quantity`, so the resulting data might have units like
    ``km/m`` if you divided for example 100km by 5m. So this Mixin has adopted
    this behavior.

    Examples
    --------
    Using this Mixin with `~astropy.nddata.NDData`:

        >>> from astropy.nddata import NDData, NDArithmeticMixin
        >>> class NDDataWithMath(NDArithmeticMixin, NDData):
        ...     pass

    Using it with one operand on an instance::

        >>> ndd = NDDataWithMath(100)
        >>> ndd.add(20)
        NDDataWithMath(120)

    Using it with two operand on an instance::

        >>> ndd = NDDataWithMath(-4)
        >>> ndd.divide(1, ndd)
        NDDataWithMath(-0.25)

    Using it as classmethod requires two operands::

        >>> NDDataWithMath.subtract(5, 4)
        NDDataWithMath(1)

    """

    def _arithmetic(
        self,
        operation,
        operand,
        propagate_uncertainties=True,
        handle_mask=np.logical_or,
        handle_meta=None,
        uncertainty_correlation=0,
        compare_wcs="first_found",
        operation_ignores_mask=False,
        axis=None,
        **kwds,
    ):
        """
        Base method which calculates the result of the arithmetic operation.

        This method determines the result of the arithmetic operation on the
        ``data`` including their units and then forwards to other methods
        to calculate the other properties for the result (like uncertainty).

        Parameters
        ----------
        operation : callable
            The operation that is performed on the `NDData`. Supported are
            `numpy.add`, `numpy.subtract`, `numpy.multiply` and
            `numpy.true_divide`.

        operand : same type (class) as self
            see :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin.add`

        propagate_uncertainties : `bool` or ``None``, optional
            see :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin.add`

        handle_mask : callable, ``'first_found'`` or ``None``, optional
            see :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin.add`

        handle_meta : callable, ``'first_found'`` or ``None``, optional
            see :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin.add`

        compare_wcs : callable, ``'first_found'`` or ``None``, optional
            see :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin.add`

        uncertainty_correlation : ``Number`` or `~numpy.ndarray`, optional
            see :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin.add`

        operation_ignores_mask : bool, optional
            When True, masked values will be excluded from operations;
            otherwise the operation will be performed on all values,
            including masked ones.

        axis : int or tuple of ints, optional
            axis or axes over which to perform collapse operations like min, max, sum or mean.

        kwargs :
            Any other parameter that should be passed to the
            different :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic_mask` (or wcs, ...)
            methods.

        Returns
        -------
        result : ndarray or `~astropy.units.Quantity`
            The resulting data as array (in case both operands were without
            unit) or as quantity if at least one had a unit.

        kwargs : `dict`
            The kwargs should contain all the other attributes (besides data
            and unit) needed to create a new instance for the result. Creating
            the new instance is up to the calling method, for example
            :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin.add`.

        """
        # Find the appropriate keywords for the appropriate method (not sure
        # if data and uncertainty are ever used ...)
        kwds2 = {"mask": {}, "meta": {}, "wcs": {}, "data": {}, "uncertainty": {}}
        for i in kwds:
            splitted = i.split("_", 1)
            try:
                kwds2[splitted[0]][splitted[1]] = kwds[i]
            except KeyError:
                raise KeyError(f"Unknown prefix {splitted[0]} for parameter {i}")

        kwargs = {}

        # First check that the WCS allows the arithmetic operation
        if compare_wcs is None:
            kwargs["wcs"] = None
        elif compare_wcs in ["ff", "first_found"]:
            if self.wcs is None and hasattr(operand, "wcs"):
                kwargs["wcs"] = deepcopy(operand.wcs)
            else:
                kwargs["wcs"] = deepcopy(self.wcs)
        else:
            kwargs["wcs"] = self._arithmetic_wcs(
                operation, operand, compare_wcs, **kwds2["wcs"]
            )

        # collapse operations on masked quantities/arrays which are supported by
        # the astropy.utils.masked or np.ma modules should use those modules to
        # do the arithmetic on the data and propagate masks.
        use_masked_arith = operand is None and self.mask is not None
        if use_masked_arith:
            # if we're *including* masked values in the operation,
            # use the astropy Masked module:
            if not operation_ignores_mask:
                # call the numpy operation on a Masked NDDataArray
                # representation of the nddata, with units when available:
                if self.unit is not None and not hasattr(self.data, "unit"):
                    masked_input = Masked(self.data << self.unit, mask=self.mask)
                else:
                    masked_input = Masked(self.data, mask=self.mask)
            # if we're *excluding* masked values in the operation,
            # we use the numpy.ma module:
            else:
                masked_input = np.ma.masked_array(self.data, self.mask)
            result = operation(masked_input, axis=axis)
            # since result may be e.g. a float if operation is a sum over all axes,
            # let's ensure that result is a masked array, since we'll assume this later:
            if not hasattr(result, "mask"):
                result = np.ma.masked_array(
                    result, mask=np.zeros_like(result, dtype=bool)
                )
        else:
            # Then calculate the resulting data (which can but needs not be a
            # quantity)
            result = self._arithmetic_data(
                operation, operand, axis=axis, **kwds2["data"]
            )

        # preserve original units
        if not hasattr(result, "unit") and hasattr(self, "unit"):
            kwargs["unit"] = self.unit

        # Determine the other properties
        if propagate_uncertainties is None:
            kwargs["uncertainty"] = None
        elif not propagate_uncertainties:
            if self.uncertainty is None:
                kwargs["uncertainty"] = deepcopy(operand.uncertainty)
            else:
                kwargs["uncertainty"] = deepcopy(self.uncertainty)
        else:
            kwargs["uncertainty"] = self._arithmetic_uncertainty(
                operation,
                operand,
                result,
                uncertainty_correlation,
                axis=axis,
                **kwds2["uncertainty"],
            )

        # If both are None, there is nothing to do.
        if self.psf is not None or (operand is not None and operand.psf is not None):
            warnings.warn(
                f"Not setting psf attribute during {operation.__name__}.",
                AstropyUserWarning,
            )

        if handle_mask is None:
            pass
        elif hasattr(result, "mask"):
            # if numpy.ma or astropy.utils.masked is being used, the constructor
            # will pick up the mask from the masked object:
            kwargs["mask"] = None
        elif handle_mask in ["ff", "first_found"]:
            if self.mask is None:
                kwargs["mask"] = deepcopy(operand.mask)
            else:
                kwargs["mask"] = deepcopy(self.mask)
        else:
            kwargs["mask"] = self._arithmetic_mask(
                operation, operand, handle_mask, axis=axis, **kwds2["mask"]
            )

        if handle_meta is None:
            kwargs["meta"] = None
        elif handle_meta in ["ff", "first_found"]:
            if not self.meta:
                kwargs["meta"] = deepcopy(operand.meta)
            else:
                kwargs["meta"] = deepcopy(self.meta)
        else:
            kwargs["meta"] = self._arithmetic_meta(
                operation, operand, handle_meta, **kwds2["meta"]
            )

        # Wrap the individual results into a new instance of the same class.
        return result, kwargs

    def _arithmetic_data(self, operation, operand, **kwds):
        """
        Calculate the resulting data.

        Parameters
        ----------
        operation : callable
            see `NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic` parameter description.

        operand : `NDData`-like instance
            The second operand wrapped in an instance of the same class as
            self.

        kwds :
            Additional parameters.

        Returns
        -------
        result_data : ndarray or `~astropy.units.Quantity`
            If both operands had no unit the resulting data is a simple numpy
            array, but if any of the operands had a unit the return is a
            Quantity.
        """
        # Do the calculation with or without units
        if self.unit is None:
            if operand.unit is None:
                result = operation(self.data, operand.data)
            else:
                result = operation(
                    self.data << dimensionless_unscaled, operand.data << operand.unit
                )
        elif hasattr(operand, "unit"):
            if operand.unit is not None:
                result = operation(self.data << self.unit, operand.data << operand.unit)
            else:
                result = operation(
                    self.data << self.unit, operand.data << dimensionless_unscaled
                )
        elif operand is not None:
            result = operation(self.data << self.unit, operand.data << operand.unit)
        else:
            result = operation(self.data, axis=kwds["axis"])

        return result

    def _arithmetic_uncertainty(self, operation, operand, result, correlation, **kwds):
        """
        Calculate the resulting uncertainty.

        Parameters
        ----------
        operation : callable
            see :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic` parameter description.

        operand : `NDData`-like instance
            The second operand wrapped in an instance of the same class as
            self.

        result : `~astropy.units.Quantity` or `~numpy.ndarray`
            The result of :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic_data`.

        correlation : number or `~numpy.ndarray`
            see :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin.add` parameter description.

        kwds :
            Additional parameters.

        Returns
        -------
        result_uncertainty : `NDUncertainty` subclass instance or None
            The resulting uncertainty already saved in the same `NDUncertainty`
            subclass that ``self`` had (or ``operand`` if self had no
            uncertainty). ``None`` only if both had no uncertainty.
        """
        # Make sure these uncertainties are NDUncertainties so this kind of
        # propagation is possible.
        if self.uncertainty is not None and not isinstance(
            self.uncertainty, NDUncertainty
        ):
            raise TypeError(
                "Uncertainty propagation is only defined for "
                "subclasses of NDUncertainty."
            )
        if (
            operand is not None
            and operand.uncertainty is not None
            and not isinstance(operand.uncertainty, NDUncertainty)
        ):
            raise TypeError(
                "Uncertainty propagation is only defined for "
                "subclasses of NDUncertainty."
            )

        # Now do the uncertainty propagation
        # TODO: There is no enforced requirement that actually forbids the
        # uncertainty to have negative entries but with correlation the
        # sign of the uncertainty DOES matter.
        if self.uncertainty is None and (
            not hasattr(operand, "uncertainty") or operand.uncertainty is None
        ):
            # Neither has uncertainties so the result should have none.
            return None
        elif self.uncertainty is None:
            # Create a temporary uncertainty to allow uncertainty propagation
            # to yield the correct results. (issue #4152)
            self.uncertainty = operand.uncertainty.__class__(None)
            result_uncert = self.uncertainty.propagate(
                operation, operand, result, correlation
            )
            # Delete the temporary uncertainty again.
            self.uncertainty = None
            return result_uncert

        elif operand is not None and operand.uncertainty is None:
            # As with self.uncertainty is None but the other way around.
            operand.uncertainty = self.uncertainty.__class__(None)
            result_uncert = self.uncertainty.propagate(
                operation, operand, result, correlation
            )
            operand.uncertainty = None
            return result_uncert

        else:
            # Both have uncertainties so just propagate.

            # only supply the axis kwarg if one has been specified for a collapsing operation
            axis_kwarg = dict(axis=kwds["axis"]) if "axis" in kwds else dict()
            return self.uncertainty.propagate(
                operation, operand, result, correlation, **axis_kwarg
            )

    def _arithmetic_mask(self, operation, operand, handle_mask, axis=None, **kwds):
        """
        Calculate the resulting mask.

        This is implemented as the piecewise ``or`` operation if both have a
        mask.

        Parameters
        ----------
        operation : callable
            see :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic` parameter description.
            By default, the ``operation`` will be ignored.

        operand : `NDData`-like instance
            The second operand wrapped in an instance of the same class as
            self.

        handle_mask : callable
            see :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin.add`

        kwds :
            Additional parameters given to ``handle_mask``.

        Returns
        -------
        result_mask : any type
            If only one mask was present this mask is returned.
            If neither had a mask ``None`` is returned. Otherwise
            ``handle_mask`` must create (and copy) the returned mask.
        """
        # If only one mask is present we need not bother about any type checks
        if (
            self.mask is None and operand is not None and operand.mask is None
        ) or handle_mask is None:
            return None
        elif self.mask is None and operand is not None:
            # Make a copy so there is no reference in the result.
            return deepcopy(operand.mask)
        elif operand is None:
            return deepcopy(self.mask)
        else:
            # Now lets calculate the resulting mask (operation enforces copy)
            return handle_mask(self.mask, operand.mask, **kwds)

    def _arithmetic_wcs(self, operation, operand, compare_wcs, **kwds):
        """
        Calculate the resulting wcs.

        There is actually no calculation involved but it is a good place to
        compare wcs information of both operands. This is currently not working
        properly with `~astropy.wcs.WCS` (which is the suggested class for
        storing as wcs property) but it will not break it neither.

        Parameters
        ----------
        operation : callable
            see :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic` parameter description.
            By default, the ``operation`` will be ignored.

        operand : `NDData` instance or subclass
            The second operand wrapped in an instance of the same class as
            self.

        compare_wcs : callable
            see :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin.add` parameter description.

        kwds :
            Additional parameters given to ``compare_wcs``.

        Raises
        ------
        ValueError
            If ``compare_wcs`` returns ``False``.

        Returns
        -------
        result_wcs : any type
            The ``wcs`` of the first operand is returned.
        """
        # ok, not really arithmetic but we need to check which wcs makes sense
        # for the result and this is an ideal place to compare the two WCS,
        # too.

        # I'll assume that the comparison returned None or False in case they
        # are not equal.
        if not compare_wcs(self.wcs, operand.wcs, **kwds):
            raise ValueError("WCS are not equal.")

        return deepcopy(self.wcs)

    def _arithmetic_meta(self, operation, operand, handle_meta, **kwds):
        """
        Calculate the resulting meta.

        Parameters
        ----------
        operation : callable
            see :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic` parameter description.
            By default, the ``operation`` will be ignored.

        operand : `NDData`-like instance
            The second operand wrapped in an instance of the same class as
            self.

        handle_meta : callable
            see :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin.add`

        kwds :
            Additional parameters given to ``handle_meta``.

        Returns
        -------
        result_meta : any type
            The result of ``handle_meta``.
        """
        # Just return what handle_meta does with both of the metas.
        return handle_meta(self.meta, operand.meta, **kwds)

    @sharedmethod
    @format_doc(_arit_doc, name="addition", op="+")
    def add(self, operand, operand2=None, **kwargs):
        return self._prepare_then_do_arithmetic(np.add, operand, operand2, **kwargs)

    @sharedmethod
    @format_doc(_arit_doc, name="subtraction", op="-")
    def subtract(self, operand, operand2=None, **kwargs):
        return self._prepare_then_do_arithmetic(
            np.subtract, operand, operand2, **kwargs
        )

    @sharedmethod
    @format_doc(_arit_doc, name="multiplication", op="*")
    def multiply(self, operand, operand2=None, **kwargs):
        return self._prepare_then_do_arithmetic(
            np.multiply, operand, operand2, **kwargs
        )

    @sharedmethod
    @format_doc(_arit_doc, name="division", op="/")
    def divide(self, operand, operand2=None, **kwargs):
        return self._prepare_then_do_arithmetic(
            np.true_divide, operand, operand2, **kwargs
        )

    @sharedmethod
    def sum(self, **kwargs):
        return self._prepare_then_do_arithmetic(np.sum, **kwargs)

    @sharedmethod
    def mean(self, **kwargs):
        return self._prepare_then_do_arithmetic(np.mean, **kwargs)

    @sharedmethod
    def min(self, **kwargs):
        # use the provided propagate_uncertainties if available, otherwise default is False:
        propagate_uncertainties = kwargs.pop("propagate_uncertainties", None)
        return self._prepare_then_do_arithmetic(
            np.min, propagate_uncertainties=propagate_uncertainties, **kwargs
        )

    @sharedmethod
    def max(self, **kwargs):
        # use the provided propagate_uncertainties if available, otherwise default is False:
        propagate_uncertainties = kwargs.pop("propagate_uncertainties", None)
        return self._prepare_then_do_arithmetic(
            np.max, propagate_uncertainties=propagate_uncertainties, **kwargs
        )

    @sharedmethod
    def _prepare_then_do_arithmetic(
        self_or_cls, operation, operand=None, operand2=None, **kwargs
    ):
        """Intermediate method called by public arithmetic (i.e. ``add``)
        before the processing method (``_arithmetic``) is invoked.

        .. warning::
            Do not override this method in subclasses.

        This method checks if it was called as instance or as class method and
        then wraps the operands and the result from ``_arithmetic`` in the
        appropriate subclass.

        Parameters
        ----------
        self_or_cls : instance or class
            ``sharedmethod`` behaves like a normal method if called on the
            instance (then this parameter is ``self``) but like a classmethod
            when called on the class (then this parameter is ``cls``).

        operations : callable
            The operation (normally a numpy-ufunc) that represents the
            appropriate action.

        operand, operand2, kwargs :
            See for example ``add``.

        Result
        ------
        result : `~astropy.nddata.NDData`-like
            Depending how this method was called either ``self_or_cls``
            (called on class) or ``self_or_cls.__class__`` (called on instance)
            is the NDData-subclass that is used as wrapper for the result.
        """
        # DO NOT OVERRIDE THIS METHOD IN SUBCLASSES.

        if isinstance(self_or_cls, NDArithmeticMixin):
            # True means it was called on the instance, so self_or_cls is
            # a reference to self
            cls = self_or_cls.__class__
            if operand2 is None:
                # Only one operand was given. Set operand2 to operand and
                # operand to self so that we call the appropriate method of the
                # operand.
                operand2 = operand
                operand = self_or_cls
            else:
                # Convert the first operand to the class of this method.
                # This is important so that always the correct _arithmetics is
                # called later that method.
                operand = cls(operand)

        else:
            # It was used as classmethod so self_or_cls represents the cls
            cls = self_or_cls

            # It was called on the class so we expect two operands!
            if operand2 is None:
                raise TypeError(
                    "operand2 must be given when the method isn't "
                    "called on an instance."
                )

            # Convert to this class. See above comment why.
            operand = cls(operand)

        # At this point operand, operand2, kwargs and cls are determined.
        if operand2 is not None and not issubclass(
            operand2.__class__, NDArithmeticMixin
        ):
            # Let's try to convert operand2 to the class of operand to allow for
            # arithmetic operations with numbers, lists, numpy arrays, numpy masked
            # arrays, astropy quantities, masked quantities and of other subclasses
            # of NDData.
            operand2 = cls(operand2)

            # Now call the _arithmetics method to do the arithmetic.
            result, init_kwds = operand._arithmetic(operation, operand2, **kwargs)
        elif issubclass(operand2.__class__, NDArithmeticMixin):
            # calling as class method:
            result, init_kwds = cls._arithmetic(
                operand,
                operation,
                operand2,
                **kwargs,
            )
        else:
            # otherwise call the _arithmetic method on self for a collapse operation:
            # for collapse operations, use astropy.utils.masked rather than handle_mask
            result, init_kwds = self_or_cls._arithmetic(
                operation,
                operand2,
                **kwargs,
            )

        # Return a new class based on the result
        return cls(result, **init_kwds)

    """Implements `NDData` with all Mixins.

    This class implements a `NDData`-like container that supports reading and
    writing as implemented in the ``astropy.io.registry`` and also slicing
    (indexing) and simple arithmetic (add, subtract, divide and multiply).

    Notes
    -----
    A key distinction from `NDDataArray` is that this class does not attempt
    to provide anything that was not defined in any of the parent classes.

    See Also
    --------
    NDData
    NDArithmeticMixin
    NDSlicingMixin
    NDIOMixin

    Examples
    --------
    The mixins allow operation that are not possible with `NDData` or
    `NDDataBase`, i.e. simple arithmetic::

        >>> from astropy.nddata import NDDataRef, StdDevUncertainty
        >>> import numpy as np

        >>> data = np.ones((3,3), dtype=float)
        >>> ndd1 = NDDataRef(data, uncertainty=StdDevUncertainty(data))
        >>> ndd2 = NDDataRef(data, uncertainty=StdDevUncertainty(data))

        >>> ndd3 = ndd1.add(ndd2)
        >>> ndd3.data  # doctest: +FLOAT_CMP
        array([[2., 2., 2.],
               [2., 2., 2.],
               [2., 2., 2.]])
        >>> ndd3.uncertainty.array  # doctest: +FLOAT_CMP
        array([[1.41421356, 1.41421356, 1.41421356],
               [1.41421356, 1.41421356, 1.41421356],
               [1.41421356, 1.41421356, 1.41421356]])

    see `NDArithmeticMixin` for a complete list of all supported arithmetic
    operations.

    But also slicing (indexing) is possible::

        >>> ndd4 = ndd3[1,:]
        >>> ndd4.data  # doctest: +FLOAT_CMP
        array([2., 2., 2.])
        >>> ndd4.uncertainty.array  # doctest: +FLOAT_CMP
        array([1.41421356, 1.41421356, 1.41421356])

    See `NDSlicingMixin` for a description how slicing works (which attributes)
    are sliced.

Observation: {"node": "astropy/nddata/mixins/ndarithmetic.py:NDArithmeticMixin", "code": "class NDArithmeticMixin:
    """
    Mixin class to add arithmetic to an NDData object.

    When subclassing, be sure to list the superclasses in the correct order
    so that the subclass sees NDData as the main superclass. See
    `~astropy.nddata.NDDataArray` for an example.

    Notes
    -----
    This class only aims at covering the most common cases so there are certain
    restrictions on the saved attributes::

        - ``uncertainty`` : has to be something that has a `NDUncertainty`-like
          interface for uncertainty propagation
        - ``mask`` : has to be something that can be used by a bitwise ``or``
          operation.
        - ``wcs`` : has to implement a way of comparing with ``=`` to allow
          the operation.

    But there is a workaround that allows to disable handling a specific
    attribute and to simply set the results attribute to ``None`` or to
    copy the existing attribute (and neglecting the other).
    For example for uncertainties not representing an `NDUncertainty`-like
    interface you can alter the ``propagate_uncertainties`` parameter in
    :meth:`NDArithmeticMixin.add`. ``None`` means that the result will have no
    uncertainty, ``False`` means it takes the uncertainty of the first operand
    (if this does not exist from the second operand) as the result's
    uncertainty. This behavior is also explained in the docstring for the
    different arithmetic operations.

    Decomposing the units is not attempted, mainly due to the internal mechanics
    of `~astropy.units.Quantity`, so the resulting data might have units like
    ``km/m`` if you divided for example 100km by 5m. So this Mixin has adopted
    this behavior.

    Examples
    --------
    Using this Mixin with `~astropy.nddata.NDData`:

        >>> from astropy.nddata import NDData, NDArithmeticMixin
        >>> class NDDataWithMat
...[truncated]...

Step 2 — fetch the suspected code (why)
- I fetched the NDDataRef class (to confirm it uses the arithmetic mixin).
  - Expectation: NDDataRef is a thin wrapper that inherits NDArithmeticMixin, so the arithmetic behaviour will be in NDArithmeticMixin.

What I inspected (key findings)
- NDDataRef is a class inheriting NDArithmeticMixin (so arithmetic is implemented in the mixin).
- NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic orchestrates the arithmetic and decides how to set the result mask. Relevant excerpt (lines numbers as in file):
  - At the end of _arithmetic it decides kwargs["mask"] either by:
    - if handle_mask is None -> do nothing
    - elif hasattr(result, "mask") -> kwargs["mask"]=None (masked result handled by masked object)
    - elif handle_mask in ["ff", "first_found"] -> copy the first found mask
    - else -> kwargs["mask"] = self._arithmetic_mask(...)
- The implementation of _arithmetic_mask (lines ~485-527) is:

  - If (self.mask is None and operand is not None and operand.mask is None) or handle_mask is None:
      return None
  - elif self.mask is None and operand is not None:
      return deepcopy(operand.mask)
  - elif operand is None:
      return deepcopy(self.mask)
  - else:
      return handle_mask(self.mask, operand.mask, **kwds)

Analysis of how this leads to the TypeError reported
- In your repro: nref_mask.multiply(1., handle_mask=np.bitwise_or)
  - When calling multiply on the instance (nref_mask), the one-operand logic in _prepare_then_do_arithmetic sets:
    - operand = self (nref_mask)
    - operand2 = 1. -> converted to NDDataRef(1.) (call it other). This other has mask == None.
  - Inside _arithmetic, self.mask is the mask array (not None), and operand (the other NDDataRef) is not None but operand.mask is None.
  - _arithmetic_mask reaches the final else branch (because none of the earlier branches matched) and calls handle_mask(self.mask, operand.mask), i.e. np.bitwise_or(mask_array, None).
  - np.bitwise_or attempts to do elementwise bitwise OR between integers and None -> TypeError unsupported operand types for |: 'int' and 'NoneType' (this is exactly the error you reported).
- So the root cause is that _arithmetic_mask only handles the case where self.mask is None (copy operand.mask) but does not symmetrically handle the case where operand.mask is None and self.mask is present. The last else branch blindly calls handle_mask even when one of the masks is None.

Primary buggy method
- NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic_mask (in astropy/nddata/mixins/ndarithmetic.py) — the condition logic does not handle the case where one operand has a mask and the other operand exists but has mask == None, causing a callable handle_mask to be invoked with a None operand.

Proposed fix direction (concrete)
- Make _arithmetic_mask symmetric: when exactly one of the operands has a mask, return a deepcopy of that mask instead of calling handle_mask with None. Only call handle_mask when both operands have non-None masks.
- Minimal change (conceptual patch) — replace the current body with logic similar to:

  - If handle_mask is None: return None
  - If operand is None:
      return deepcopy(self.mask)
  - If self.mask is None and (operand is not None and operand.mask is None):
      return None
  - If self.mask is None and operand is not None:
      return deepcopy(operand.mask)
  - If operand is not None and getattr(operand, "mask", None) is None and self.mask is not None:
      return deepcopy(self.mask)
  - Else (both masks present):
      return handle_mask(self.mask, operand.mask, **kwds)

- A compact replacement that preserves the original intended branches is:

    def _arithmetic_mask(self, operation, operand, handle_mask, axis=None, **kwds):
        # If a mask handler was explicitly disabled, return None
        if handle_mask is None:
            return None

        # If there is no second operand (collapse) -> return copy of self.mask
        if operand is None:
            return deepcopy(self.mask)

        # At this point operand is not None.
        mask_self = self.mask
        mask_operand = getattr(operand, "mask", None)

        # If neither has a mask -> None
        if mask_self is None and mask_operand is None:
            return None

        # If only operand has a mask -> copy it
        if mask_self is None and mask_operand is not None:
            return deepcopy(mask_operand)

        # If only self has a mask -> copy it
        if mask_self is not None and mask_operand is None:
            return deepcopy(mask_self)

        # Both have masks -> use handle_mask to combine them
        return handle_mask(mask_self, mask_operand, **kwds)

Why this fixes the problem
- This ensures handle_mask is only called when both masks are present and avoids calling e.g. np.bitwise_or with a None which causes the TypeError. It also keeps the intended behavior that if only one mask exists, that mask is copied to the result (matching the behavior described in expected behavior and what older versions evidently did).

Secondary issues / likely related occurrences
- I searched the codebase for other similar asymmetric checks (e.g., "operand.mask is None") and found no other matches. So this is likely the single primary occurrence of this specific faulty pattern.
- There is a small potential for confusion with the branch where operand is None (collapse operations). The current code had the branch "elif operand is None: return deepcopy(self.mask)". The proposed logic preserves this behavior.
- Ensure this fix doesn't interfere with the earlier logic in _arithmetic that sets kwargs["mask"] = None when the data result is a masked object (i.e., when hasattr(result, "mask") is True). That behavior is unchanged.

Test suggestions (to validate the fix)
- Add or run tests covering these matrix of cases with handle_mask=np.bitwise_or (and possibly handle_mask=np.logical_or):
  - both operands have mask -> combined via handle_mask (existing passing tests)
  - neither operand has mask -> result mask is None (existing passing tests)
  - self has mask, operand has no mask -> result is copy of self.mask (this was failing)
  - operand has mask, self has no mask -> result is copy of operand.mask
  - operand is None (collapse) with self.mask not None -> copy of self.mask (existing branch)

What else to consider (if this fix is applied)
- Calling handle_mask only when both masks present is consistent with the docstring: "If only one mask was present this mask is returned." So changing the code to exactly implement that should be safe.
- There could be callers that passed handle_mask callables explicitly expecting to be called even if one argument is None (unlikely and contrary to the docstring). If someone relied on handle_mask to accept None, this change would alter behavior (but that would be unusual and arguably incorrect usage).
- The change is localized to NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic_mask. No other methods in the same file appear to have the pattern that leads to this bug, per search.
- Consider adding unit tests to prevent regression: ensure cases where one mask is None return copied mask and not attempt to call handle_mask.

Summary (final)
- Root cause: NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic_mask does not handle the case where self.mask is present and operand.mask is None, so it calls handle_mask(self.mask, None) causing the TypeError you observed.
- Primary fix: update _arithmetic_mask to explicitly detect when exactly one operand's mask is None and return a deepcopy of the other mask, only invoking handle_mask when both masks are present.
- This is a small, local, and symmetric bugfix that matches the documented/described behavior and restores the expected behaviour reported as working in v5.2.

    - Handles performing the operation with correct units and broadcasting; returns result array or Quantity.
  - _arithmetic_uncertainty(self, operation, operand, result, correlation, **kwds)
    - Handles uncertainty propagation using NDUncertainty API.
  - _arithmetic_mask(self, operation, operand, handle_mask, axis=None, **kwds)
    - The method identified as buggy. Current implementation:
      - If (self.mask is None and operand is not None and operand.mask is None) or handle_mask is None: return None
      - elif self.mask is None and operand is not None: return deepcopy(operand.mask)
      - elif operand is None: return deepcopy(self.mask)
      - else: return handle_mask(self.mask, operand.mask, **kwds)
    - Docstring states: "If only one mask was present this mask is returned. If neither had a mask None is returned. Otherwise handle_mask must create (and copy) the returned mask."
  - _arithmetic_wcs(self, operation, operand, compare_wcs, **kwds)
    - Uses compare_wcs (which can be 'first_found' shorthand "ff") to pick and copy the WCS from operands; may raise ValueError if compare_wcs returns False.
  - _arithmetic_meta(self, operation, operand, handle_meta, **kwds)
    - Calls handle_meta(self.meta, operand.meta, **kwds).
  - add, subtract, multiply, divide: wrappers calling _prepare_then_do_arithmetic with numpy ufuncs (np.add, etc).
  - sum, mean, min, max: wrappers too.
  - _prepare_then_do_arithmetic(self_or_cls, operation, operand=None, operand2=None, **kwargs)
    - Sharedmethod that sets up operands and calls _arithmetic appropriately; also constructs return instance from returned result and kwargs.

Implications for the fix's scope
- NDArithmeticMixin is the central place for arithmetic and mask handling. Changing _arithmetic_mask will directly affect how masks are determined in arithmetic operations for all subclasses that use this mixin (NDDataRef, NDDataArray, CCDData, etc.). Because _arithmetic decides whether to call _arithmetic_mask (or to use "first_found" or masked arrays), any change needs to preserve overall semantics used in other branches (e.g., collapse behavior, "first_found", handle_mask None, and masked-array result handling).
- The mixin is used widely; a change here will affect multiple higher-level classes.

Tool calls and findings

- The tests we previously opened (test_ndarithmetic.py) include extensive tests for mask combinations, including the expectation that combining mask None with a mask returns the mask (i.e., copy). Those tests also covered using a custom handle_mask callable that expects to be called with two masks. They assume handle_mask callable will be used only when both masks are present (the meaningful case) or that handle_mask can accept both present masks; they don't require handle_mask to accept None.

Other places operating on masks:
- NDDataArray.__array__: returns np.ma.masked_array(self.data, self.mask) when mask is not None. That is, code expects that when mask is present (exposed via property), it can be passed to numpy.ma.masked_array. So the mask should be a boolean ndarray or something acceptable to numpy.ma.
- NDSlicingMixin._slice_mask: returns self.mask[item] when self.mask is not None, with try/except TypeError for non-sliceable masks. So mask slicing is expected to work for mask types that are array-like.
- CCDData.to_hdu: expects mask to have 'shape' and converts it to uint8 before writing. So mask being None must be bypassed; mask must be an array to be written.

Implications
- The public mask property should be None or an array-like mask that the consumer can use. That aligns with NDArithmeticMixin's documented expectations.
- A fix should ensure the result mask returned by _arithmetic_mask is either None or a copy (deepcopy) of a mask array or result of handle_mask when both masks present. It should not call handle_mask with None.

Step 5 — Check callers and consumers
What I looked for and why
- I want to find what calls NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic or _arithmetic_mask. This tells us how the method is used and what callers expect (e.g., _arithmetic sets kwargs["mask"] to returned value; then the returned kwargs are passed to class constructor, so the constructor's mask parameter must accept the returned type).

Tool calls / what I found

- _prepare_then_do_arithmetic does conversions to cls(operand) for operand values that are not NDArithmeticMixin subclass instances. For scalars (like 1.), operand2 becomes cls(1.) where cls is the class on which the method was called (NDDataRef for instance operations).
- Therefore, when user does nref_mask.multiply(1., handle_mask=np.bitwise_or), operand2 is converted into NDDataRef(1.) — which sets mask = None (unless a mask is provided). So operand.mask is None as observed by user. In _arithmetic, self.mask is an array (since nref_mask had a mask), and operand.mask is None. _arithmetic invokes _arithmetic_mask with handle_mask=np.bitwise_or; inside _arithmetic_mask the code falls into the final else branch and calls handle_mask(self.mask, operand.mask) -> np.bitwise_or(mask_array, None) -> raises TypeError.
- The only callers of _arithmetic_mask are from within NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic (kwargs["mask"] = self._arithmetic_mask(...)). No other external call sites.

Implications
- The failing scenario is created by _prepare_then_do_arithmetic converting scalars/values to NDDataRef instances with mask None. So in pairwise arithmetic, it's common for one operand to have a mask and the other not. _arithmetic_mask must handle this robustly (copy the non-None mask) rather than calling handle_mask with None.

    - Main orchestrator. Steps:

    - Intended behavior per docstring: "If only one mask was present this mask is returned. If neither had a mask None is returned. Otherwise handle_mask must create (and copy) the returned mask."
    - Current implementation:

      - elif self.mask is None and operand is not None: return deepcopy(operand.mask)
      - elif operand is None: return deepcopy(self.mask)
      - else: return handle_mask(self.mask, operand.mask, **kwds)

    - sum, mean, min, max: collapse operations

    - Sharedmethod wrapper that handles being called as instance or classmethod, converts operands to cls instances, then calls operand._arithmetic (or cls._arithmetic for classmethod usage); finally returns new instance: return cls(result, **init_kwds)

- NDArithmeticMixin is the canonical implementation for arithmetic and mask merging. Sibling classes (NDDataArray, NDDataRef, CCDData) rely on it; they do not override _arithmetic_mask, though they may normalize internal mask representation.
- NDDataArray normalizes mask input to boolean ndarray and converts None to np.ma.nomask internally; NDData leaves mask as-is. Both present a property mask that returns None or the mask array. NDArithmeticMixin accesses the public property mask and operates on those values — so understanding both underlying representations is important, but public API is consistent.

- NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic -> for mask handling:
  - If handle_mask is None: do nothing; result mask should be un-set by mixin (the class constructor will treat missing mask accordingly).
  - If result has .mask attr (result is masked object): set kwargs["mask"] to None; constructor should pick up mask from result masked object.
  - If handle_mask == 'first_found'/'ff': copy first found mask (prefer self.mask unless self.mask is None).
  - Else: call self._arithmetic_mask(...) which is intended to return None if no masks present, a deepcopy of single mask if only one present, or call handle_mask to produce new mask when both present.

- _arithmetic_mask code path:
  - Cases:
    - operand is None: collapse operation -> returns copy of self.mask (deepcopy)
    - self.mask None and operand not None:
      - if operand.mask is None -> returns None
      - else -> deepcopy(operand.mask)
    - Current bug: when self.mask not None and operand.mask is None (both operand is not None), the code does not have explicit branch for this, ends up in final else branch calling handle_mask(self.mask, operand.mask) where operand.mask is None -> causes error when handle_mask is numpy ufunc.
  - Special case in _arithmetic: use_masked_arith = operand is None and self.mask is not None -> switched to Masked/np.ma for collapse operations; this is separate from handle_mask path.

- Slicing and constructor:
  - NDSlicingMixin._slice_mask uses mask property and slicing; small chance that changes might affect compatibility but fix is local to mask merging.
  - NDData constructor: if an NDData-like object is provided, it may copy over mask attribute if not explicitly provided; but _arithmetic returns (result, kwargs) which are passed to cls(result, **init_kwds) by _prepare_then_do_arithmetic, so the mask returned by _arithmetic_mask ends up passed as the "mask" parameter to class constructor.

5) Any hardcoded values or constants that encode assumptions
- handle_mask default np.logical_or and its use as default behavior for combining masks.
- 'ff'/'first_found' behavior: copy first found mask (mix-in implements this behavior in _arithmetic).
- In tests and docstrings the semantics are spelled out: if only one mask present, return that mask; if none -> None; if both -> combine via provided function.
- The code in _arithmetic_mask incorrectly exercises handle_mask with None in at least one branch — this violates the assumption that handle_mask will only be invoked when both masks are present.

Conclusions from this investigation (without proposing fixes)
- Root cause confirmed: NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic_mask's branching condition does not handle the case where self.mask is present and operand.mask is None (i.e., self.mask != None and operand.mask == None). In that scenario, the current code calls handle_mask(self.mask, None) (e.g., np.bitwise_or(mask_array, None)) which raises TypeError.
- The intended semantics — per docstring and tests — are:
  - If both masks are None => return None
  - If only one mask present => return a copy of that mask
  - If both masks present => return handle_mask(mask1, mask2, **kwds)
- The change necessary is to make _arithmetic_mask symmetric: only call handle_mask when both masks are non-None; return deepcopy of whichever mask exists when exactly one is present; return None when neither present or handle_mask is None.
- The change is local to NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic_mask; however, because NDArithmeticMixin is used by NDDataRef, NDDataArray, CCDData, and any other NDData subclasses using this mixin, behavior will change for arithmetic across library; tests should be updated/confirmed accordingly.
- The NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic also has branches where kwargs["mask"] is set to None if the data operation produced a masked object (i.e., result has .mask). This behavior must remain unchanged.
- Tests already exist that assert the intended behavior (test_arithmetics_data_masks) — these tests likely pass in the repo, but the user reported that behavior changed between v5.2 and v5.3. The code in _arithmetic_mask suggests a logic error was introduced causing the handling asymmetry.
