Tool call 1 — get_file_context(astropy/wcs/wcs.py lines 1-2000)
- Why I ran it: to read the WCS class file from the top through the core of the class so I can enumerate all methods defined there (this maps the affected class completely).
- What I found (high level):
  - The file is astropy/wcs/wcs.py. It imports the C extension _wcs and pulls WCSBase, DistortionLookupTable, Sip, Wcsprm, Tabprm and many exception classes from it if available (lines ~46–101). If _wcs is not available, the module defines placeholders.
  - WCS inherits from WCSBase (line ~215: class WCS(WCSBase):). WCSBase is the C extension class _wcs._Wcs (if extension present).
  - The WCS.__init__ sets up self.wcs (Wcsprm object), self.sip, cpdis, det2im, self.naxis etc.
  - Important C-extension components are attached as module-level names: WCSBase, DistortionLookupTable, Sip, Wcsprm, Tabprm, and many exceptions (lines ~86–100).
  - Key helper functions and methods we care about are present: _denormalize_sky, _normalize_sky, _array_converter (the dispatcher that is the target for the proposed change), all_pix2world, wcs_pix2world, _all_world2pix, all_world2pix, wcs_world2pix, pix2foc, p4_pix2foc, det2im, sip_pix2foc, sip_foc2pix, and many other utility methods.
- Implication: I now have the authoritative source for all WCS methods (at least the Python code). The central dispatcher _array_converter is defined here and is the right place to implement a short-circuit for zero-length inputs. But because WCS relies on C extension methods (p2s, s2p, etc.) we must also find any code that calls those C methods directly and would still be vulnerable to zero-length array inputs.

What I learned that's directly relevant to the bug:
- The traceback in the bug report shows a chain: wcs_pix2world -> _array_converter -> _return_list_of_arrays -> call to func(xy, origin) (func is lambda xy, o: self.wcs.p2s(xy, o)['world']). _array_converter constructs xy even when xy has zero rows (N == 0), then passes it to wcslib via self.wcs.p2s -> wcslib errors on ncoord==0. The current code has no early-return for xy.shape[0] == 0.
- The two inner helpers inside _array_converter: _return_list_of_arrays(axes, origin) and _return_single_array(xy, origin) both call func(xy, origin) unconditionally after broadcast/denormalize. So an early-return needs to be implemented in both helpers (or around them).
- Many high-level transformation methods call _array_converter, so a single change there will affect many public functions (good for single fix point), but code that calls low-level .wcs.p2s / .wcs.s2p directly (bypassing _array_converter) will not be protected by such a change.

Tool call 2 — get_file_context(astropy/wcs/wcs.py lines 2001-3400)
- Why I ran it: to read the remainder of the file and ensure I capture all WCS methods, private helpers, and uses of the transformation outputs — especially the iterative solver _all_world2pix which does reductions (np.sum, np.nanmax) that may blow up if given empty arrays after we change _array_converter.
- What I found (high level):
  - The remainder of the file contains the end of _all_world2pix and the rest of the WCS class (many utility functions), plus helper functions like find_all_wcs and validate. The iterative solver _all_world2pix (lines ~1418–1814 of the file, read earlier) uses reductions such as np.sum(..., axis=1) and np.nanmax(dn) — these will error when passed empty arrays leading to zero-size reductions (ValueError).
  - WCS methods that call _array_converter are defined around the file (all_pix2world, wcs_pix2world, all_world2pix, wcs_world2pix, pix2foc, p4_pix2foc, det2im, sip_pix2foc, sip_foc2pix).
  - WCS uses self.wcs (the Wcsprm C extension object) for core transformations; e.g., wcs_pix2world uses: lambda xy,o: self.wcs.p2s(xy, o)['world'].
- Implication:
  - Simply making _array_converter return empty arrays for zero-length inputs will avoid calling wcslib and prevent the C-level InconsistentAxisTypesError. However, we must also examine other functions that take those results as inputs and perform reductions or indexing that assumes at least one row. The most critical is _all_world2pix: it calls self.wcs_world2pix(world, origin) and then, if corrections are present, continues with iterative operations that call pix2foc and do reductions. If pix0 is empty, later steps like np.sum(dpix*dpix, axis=1) and np.nanmax(dn) will raise errors; therefore _all_world2pix must check for zero-length pix0 and return early (pix0) when pix0.shape[0] == 0.
  - Similarly, other methods that perform axis-1 reductions or np.max/np.sum should be audited (but _all_world2pix is the obvious one).

Tool call 3 — get_code(astropy/wcs/docstrings.py)
- Why I ran it: _wcs (the C extension) is used heavily. The docstrings file contains precise descriptions of C extension API functions (p2s/s2p/pix2foc/p2s, etc.) and their return shapes. I wanted to confirm expected output shapes (i.e., how many columns these functions return) and other semantics so I can pick what to return for empty inputs without calling the C extension.
- What I found:
  - The docstrings describe the C extension functions precisely: p2s/pix2foc/p2s/s2p/pix2foc return double array[ncoord][nelem] — i.e., a 2D array with shape (ncoord, nelem), with nelem = number of coordinate elements (normally equal to self.naxis).
  - p2s returns a dict with keys including 'world' (the world coordinate array), also returns 'phi' and 'theta' arrays for celestial projections. _array_converter only uses the 'world' part (lambda xy, o: self.wcs.p2s(xy, o)['world']) — so the function returns an array of shape (N, M) where M == naxis.
  - pix2foc, p4_pix2foc, det2im, sip_pix2foc docstrings indicate they return arrays shaped like (ncoord, nelem). For SIP, nelem will be 2 (since SIP applies to 2D), but WCS.sip_pix2foc only calls SIP if self.sip is non-None and in SIP case naxis==2 anyway (SIP/d2im are 2D-only).
- Implication:
  - The general expectation is that the number of output columns equals the WCS naxis (i.e., the number of coordinate axes) in most cases; for ra/dec normalization (ra_dec_order flag) the public API sometimes converts to/from 2-element (ra,dec) output. So in the _array_converter early-return we can infer the output width in most cases as self.naxis, or handle the ra_dec_order special-case to return two arrays accordingly. This confirms the earlier proposed fallback to self.naxis is sensible, but we must be careful with ra_dec_order and SIP special-cases.

Synthesis — complete map of the affected class/module and surrounding code

1) Primary class (astropy.wcs.WCS) — full method map with brief behavior and relevance to zero-length input handling:

- __init__(..., header=None, ...):
  - Creates/attaches C extension Wcsprm object (self.wcs), reads SIP and distortion lookup tables, sets self.naxis, self.sip, self.cpdis1/2, self.det2im1/2, and calls self.wcs.set().
  - Relevance: creates the components (self.wcs, self.sip) that will be called by transformation methods.

- _denormalize_sky(self, sky):
  - Converts (ra,dec)-ordered 2-column arrays into the internal ordering used by wcslib. Raises if WCS lacks RA/DEC.
  - Relevance: called inside _array_converter when ra_dec_order and 'input'. For empty sky arrays, this returns an array with shape (0, naxis) in else branch or returns sky directly if 2D with expected layout. It operates on sky.shape[0]; returns zero-length arrays fine.

- _normalize_sky(self, sky):
  - Converts internal wcslib world ordering back to (ra,dec) 2-column ordering. For empty inputs, it returns an array of shape (0,2). Behavior matches the needs for early-return when ra_dec_order==True.

- _array_converter(self, func, sky, *args, ra_dec_order=False):
  - Central dispatcher supporting two calling styles:
    - Two-argument style: xy (NxM array) + origin -> _return_single_array(xy, origin)
    - Multi-argument style: 1-D arrays per axis + origin -> _return_list_of_arrays(axes, origin)
  - Internal helpers:
    - _return_list_of_arrays(axes, origin)
      - broadcast_arrays axes
      - xy = hstack of axes (shape (N, n_axes))
      - if ra_dec_order and sky == 'input': xy = _denormalize_sky(xy)
      - output = func(xy, origin)  <-- current bug: calls into wcslib even when xy.shape[0] == 0
      - if ra_dec_order and sky == 'output': output = self._normalize_sky(output); return tuple of two arrays shaped like axes[0]
      - else: return [ output[:, i].reshape(axes[0].shape) for i in range(output.shape[1]) ]
    - _return_single_array(xy, origin)
      - checks xy.shape[-1] matches self.naxis
      - if ra_dec_order and sky == 'input': xy = _denormalize_sky(xy)
      - result = func(xy, origin)  <-- same issue for xy.shape[0] == 0
      - if ra_dec_order and sky == 'output': result = _normalize_sky(result)
      - return result
  - Relevance: This is the single best place to short-circuit calls that would otherwise pass zero-length arrays to wcslib. However, early returns must exactly preserve calling convention output types and shapes.

- all_pix2world(self, *args, **kwargs):
  - Calls: return self._array_converter(self._all_pix2world, 'output', *args, **kwargs)
  - all_pix2world performs full distortion corrections and WCS core transform; its func should return an NxM array with M == naxis.
  - Relevance: Will benefit from _array_converter early-return.

- wcs_pix2world(self, *args, **kwargs):
  - Calls: return self._array_converter(lambda xy,o: self.wcs.p2s(xy,o)['world'], 'output', *args, **kwargs)
  - Relevance: This is the method that raised the InconsistentAxisTypesError in the bug report. The immediate failure arises from calling self.wcs.p2s with N==0.

- _all_world2pix(self, world, origin, tolerance, maxiter, adaptive, detect_divergence, quiet):
  - Performs inversion of all_pix2world via fixed-point iterations.
  - First does pix0 = self.wcs_world2pix(world, origin) (which will now return empty arrays for zero-length input if we change _array_converter).
  - If no distortion corrections are present it returns pix0 early.
  - Otherwise proceeds with iterations and uses operations:
    - dpix = self.pix2foc(pix, origin) - pix0
    - dn = np.sum(dpix * dpix, axis=1)  <-- will raise if dpix is shape (0, n) (np.sum(axis=1) runs but returns array of length 0; subsequent np.nanmax(dn) fails)
    - while loop uses np.nanmax(dn) and np.sum(..., axis=1) repeatedly.
  - Relevance: After changing _array_converter to return empty arrays for zero-length inputs, _all_world2pix must check for an empty pix0 and return early (pix0) before performing axis-1 reductions or np.nanmax, to avoid errors on empty arrays.

- all_world2pix(self, *args, tolerance=..., maxiter=..., adaptive=..., detect_divergence=True, quiet=False, **kwargs):
  - Wraps _all_world2pix via _array_converter.
  - Relevance: same concerns as _all_world2pix.

- wcs_world2pix(self, *args, **kwargs):
  - Calls: return self._array_converter(lambda xy,o: self.wcs.s2p(xy,o)['pixcrd'], 'input', *args, **kwargs)
  - Relevance: similar to wcs_pix2world but for inverse.

- pix2foc / p4_pix2foc / det2im:
  - Each returns self._array_converter(self._pix2foc / self._p4_pix2foc / self._det2im, None, *args)
  - Relevance: these are other transformation pipelines returning (N, M) arrays; will be covered by _array_converter early-return.

- sip_pix2foc / sip_foc2pix:
  - When self.sip is None they return identity; otherwise call self._array_converter(self.sip.pix2foc / self.sip.foc2pix, None, *args)
  - Relevance: SIP is 2D-specific. Early-returns must preserve the list-vs-array return types for SIP.

- to_fits/to_header, and many other utility functions follow; they don't directly touch the coordinate transform call chain but were scanned for reductions; nothing else critical for zero-length handling surfaced.

2) Component classes and important assumptions (class-level attributes, documented behavior)

- WCSBase (alias for _wcs._Wcs) [C extension]:
  - Parent class of WCS. Provides many C-level methods (p2s, s2p, p2s/p2s return dicts containing arrays like 'world', 'pixcrd', 'phi', 'theta').
  - Important: C extension methods do not accept ncoord == 0 and will raise InconsistentAxisTypesError if called with zero-length arrays. This is the root cause of the bug report.
  - We do not change the C extension here in this mapping phase but must be aware that direct calls to _wcs functions will still raise for zero-length arrays.

- Wcsprm (alias for _wcs.Wcsprm) [C extension]:
  - Performs the core transformations via methods p2s, s2p, p2s returns dicts with 'world' etc. Returns arrays shaped (ncoord, nelem).
  - Expectations: Output array second dimension (nelem) matches number of axes (naxis).
  - Hardcoded assumptions: C-level code expects ncoord > 0 for p2s/s2p etc.

- Sip and DistortionLookupTable (C extension classes _wcs.Sip and _wcs.DistortionLookupTable):
  - Handle SIP distortions and lookup table distortions. SIP is 2D only; these methods return (N, 2) arrays (or same shape as self.naxis).
  - Hardcoded: SIP and distortions are only valid for 2D usage; many checks in WCS.__init__ enforce this.

- The docstrings in docstrings.py confirm the return array shapes for these C extension functions and so provide guidance for what Python code should return if short-circuiting.

3) Class hierarchy and siblings
- WCS inherits from WCSBase (_wcs._Wcs). WCS overrides many Python-level helpers and provides a pipeline combining Sip/DistortionLookupTable/Wcsprm. The C extension WCSBase/Wcsprm provide low-level transformations.
- There are no other sibling Python classes that inherit from WCSBase inside this module (aside from tests that subclass WCS). So changes to WCS._array_converter do not need to coordinate with sibling method implementations.

4) All operations touching the same data (pixel/world coordinate arrays)
- Methods that create or accept these arrays (and where zero-length behavior matters):
  - wcs_pix2world: public; uses lambda to call self.wcs.p2s(... )['world'] — current crash occurs here for zero-length.
  - all_pix2world: public; wrapper around self._all_pix2world.
  - wcs_world2pix: public; uses self.wcs.s2p(... )['pixcrd'].
  - all_world2pix: public; calls _all_world2pix (iterative inversion), which calls wcs_world2pix/pix2foc and performs reductions; needs early-empty handling.
  - pix2foc, p4_pix2foc, det2im, sip_pix2foc, sip_foc2pix: all are public pipelines that call into either C extension functions or self.sip methods; they will be covered by the central _array_converter early-return.
- Tests and other code:
  - astropy/modeling/tests/test_projections.py uses w.wcs.p2s(...) and w.wcs.s2p(...) directly (low-level). These do not go through _array_converter and will still error for zero-length inputs.
- Utility functions that assume at least one point:
  - _all_world2pix uses np.sum(dpix**2, axis=1) and np.nanmax(dn) — these will break for empty arrays; _all_world2pix must be adapted to check for empty pix0 early and return it.

5) Callers and consumers (call graph)
- Most public API users will call WCS.wcs_pix2world / all_pix2world / wcs_world2pix / all_world2pix.
- Low-level users/tests call self.wcs.p2s/s2p directly.
- find_all_wcs and validate create WCS objects and are not directly part of the transform call chain for zero-length inputs, but tests using find_all_wcs may exercise these transformations.

6) Hardcoded values / assumptions to watch
- The code often assumes the number of output columns equals self.naxis. In most functions this is true (core WCS transformations return nelem==naxis). The early-return logic should pick the number of outputs accordingly.
- ra_dec_order special-case: some public API methods accept ra_dec_order (or have RA/DEC normalization via flags). The behavior of returning a tuple of (ra_array, dec_array) vs. a single Nx2 array must be preserved for empty inputs. For list-of-arrays calling style with ra_dec_order and sky == 'output', existing code returns a tuple of two 1-D arrays shaped like axes[0]. So early-return must preserve that return type (tuple of arrays).
- SIP/distortions are 2D-only; when SIP is present the outputs are 2-column arrays; early-return needs to respect that mapping.
- dtype: functions return floating point arrays; empty arrays should use dtype=float to preserve callers' expectations.

7) Concrete gaps to investigate (from the previous analysis and what my mapping found)
- _array_converter::_return_list_of_arrays determines the number of returned arrays from output.shape[1] after calling func. If we short-circuit and do not call func, we must pick the correct number of output columns. For most WCS transforms, output.shape[1] == self.naxis. For ra_dec_order==True and sky == 'output', the user-visible return shape is two arrays (RA, DEC), so early-return must produce two arrays shaped like axes[0]. If any of the functions produce a different number of columns than self.naxis (unlikely for core functions), the shortcut needs a mapping of func -> output_width. From docstrings and typical usage, assuming self.naxis is correct in all these cases is reasonable.
  - Files/functions to audit to be safe:
    - self._all_pix2world, self._all_world2pix, self._pix2foc, self._p4_pix2foc, self._det2im, self.sip.pix2foc / foc2pix — per docstrings, width == naxis (or 2 for SIP).
- _all_world2pix: It performs reductions on dpix and dn that assume at least one point. If pix0 is empty after the initial self.wcs_world2pix(world, origin) call, _all_world2pix must return pix0 immediately. Otherwise, np.sum(..., axis=1) and np.nanmax(dn) will error on zero-sized arrays. So patch must add an early exit in _all_world2pix after computing pix0: if pix0.shape[0] == 0: return pix0. This change is required in addition to _array_converter changes.
- Test code and other callers calling Wcsprm.* C extension methods directly (e.g., w.wcs.p2s / s2p) will remain vulnerable. The mapping found astropy/modeling/tests/test_projections.py as an example. Actions:
  - For the minimal fix (change only public Python API), update tests to use w.wcs_pix2world/wcs_world2pix instead of calling w.wcs.p2s/wcs.s2p directly (or add guards to tests if they need to pass empty arrays).
  - For a broader fix, consider adding Python wrappers around w.wcs.* that detect zero-length inputs and return empty outputs — or change the C extension (larger scope).

  - For all_world2pix with empty input, ensure it returns empty array and does not raise.

9) Edge-cases and further audit items
- When returning list-of-arrays, the number of arrays is determined now by output.shape[1] after calling func. If short-circuited, using self.naxis as the number of outputs is likely correct for the core WCS transforms but could be wrong for transforms where output has a different width (rare). We must audit:
  - Which functions passed as func produce outputs whose column count differs from self.naxis. From docstrings and code, this doesn't appear to be the case for the functions used here.
- ra_dec_order handling: _array_converter sometimes returns a tuple (ra, dec) 1-D arrays for list-of-arrays paths when ra_dec_order and sky == 'output'. Early-returns must preserve this behavior exactly (return tuple, not list or array).
- dtype and shape for empty arrays: world/pix coordinates are floats; return dtype=float (np.float64) empty arrays to match what wcslib would normally return (floating-point arrays).
- Cases where the inputs are not 1-D arrays but broadcasted shapes: axes = np.broadcast_arrays(*axes) returns shape axes[0].shape; use that for returned array shapes (so shape is preserved even for multidimensional broadcasted call sites).
- There may be code that depends on exceptions when empty arrays are passed to the C extension — changing to return empty arrays instead of raising an InconsistentAxisTypesError is almost certainly desired (more user-friendly), but we must ensure no code relies on that exception. I did not find evidence of such reliance.

10) Concrete list of code locations to examine/change (summary)
- Primary modifications (must be coordinated):
  - astropy/wcs/wcs.py:
    - _array_converter:
      - _return_list_of_arrays: insert early-return for xy.shape[0] == 0; construct return value(s) consistent with current API.
      - _return_single_array: insert early-return for xy.shape[0] == 0; return correct empty NxM array (or Nx2 for ra_dec_order output normalization).
    - _all_world2pix:
      - After pix0 = self.wcs_world2pix(world, origin) add early-return on pix0.shape[0] == 0 to avoid np.sum/nanmax on empty arrays.
- Tests & direct users:
  - astropy/modeling/tests/test_projections.py: calls w.wcs.p2s / s2p directly; these call the C extension and would still raise for zero-length arrays. For these tests it is not necessary to change anything unless tests start validating zero-length behavior; but they are examples of code that would need changes if the policy is to make the C extension accept zero-length arrays too.
- Optional / broader-scope changes:
  - Consider adding Python-level wrappers around w.wcs.p2s and w.wcs.s2p that detect zero-length inputs and return empty outputs; this would make direct calls safer, but this is a larger-scope change (affects tests and possibly public behavior).

11) Suggested tests to add (for the patch author; included for completeness of the map)
- For each public API method that calls _array_converter:
  - wcs_pix2world([], [], origin) -> returns list/tuple of empty arrays shaped like the input axes (or tuple of empties for ra_dec_order True).
  - wcs_pix2world(np.empty((0, 2)), origin) -> returns np.empty((0, 2)) (single-array form).
  - all_pix2world variants (same tests).
  - wcs_world2pix etc. (empty input -> empty outputs).
  - all_world2pix(empty input) -> should return an empty array of shape (0, naxis) and not raise (check also when distortions are present).
- Unit test for _all_world2pix specifically: with distortions present, passing empty world array should return empty pix array without attempting iterations or reductions.

12) Final impact assessment (high level) — where the change fixes the reported bug and what remains
- Changing _array_converter to detect zero-length inputs and return appropriate empty outputs will fix the bug reported (InconsistentAxisTypesError when calling wcs.wcs_pix2world([], [], 0)).
- This change will benefit all public transformation methods that go through _array_converter, so a single fix point is good.
- _all_world2pix must be adapted to return early when pix0 is empty; otherwise iterative reductions will fail.
- Any code that calls the low-level C extension methods (e.g., w.wcs.p2s or w.wcs.s2p) directly will still raise an InconsistentAxisTypesError for zero-length inputs; those call sites must be audited and either modified to use the high-level public API or to guard empty inputs before calling the C extension. Several tests call wcslib directly; they may need updating if desired to support empty inputs.
- There are no other obvious places in WCS that perform axis-1 reductions on transformation results besides _all_world2pix; I scanned for np.sum and np.nanmax and found those inside _all_world2pix. Other methods perform element-wise operations on returned arrays and will work with zero-length arrays (NumPy respects shape (0, N) for concatenation and slicing, and _normalize/_denormalize_sky produce correct (0,2) output shapes).

Appendix — exact code regions where changes need to be added (file & approximate line numbers in the version I inspected)
- astropy/wcs/wcs.py:
  - _array_converter:
    - _return_list_of_arrays: after line constructing xy (line ~1221 in the file snapshot), before the ra_dec_order input denormalize or before the func(xy, origin) call at line ~1225, add xy emptiness check.
    - _return_single_array: before calling result = func(xy, origin) at line ~1240, add xy emptiness check.
  - _all_world2pix:
    - after pix0 = self.wcs_world2pix(world, origin) at line ~1627, add if pix0.shape[0] == 0: return pix0

I did not produce a patch here per instructions; the above gives the precise set of functions and the exact lines/places to inspect/modify to implement the fix and the places to run additional tests. This is intended to be a complete map for a subsequent agent to implement a minimal, safe change that:
- Short-circuits zero-length inputs at the public Python layer (WCS._array_converter) for both calling styles,
- Preserves API return types and shapes (including ra_dec_order behavior),
- Adds an early return in _all_world2pix to avoid reductions on empty arrays,
- And identifies tests / direct callers that will need attention if the policy is to also make direct calls to Wcsprm.* robust to zero-length inputs.

If you want, next I can:
- Produce a precise patch (diff) implementing the early-returns described above in astropy/wcs/wcs.py, plus unit tests for the listed scenarios in the test suite (and a note of tests that should be updated that currently call wcslib directly).

simulation_result.assumption_changed: The Python WCS dispatcher (_array_converter) can call the underlying wcslib C routines with ncoord == 0; the fix would change that by short-circuiting zero-length coordinate inputs and returning appropriately-shaped empty outputs without calling the C extension.

proposed_fix.change:
After broadcasting the input 1-D axis arrays and building xy (i.e. after 'xy = np.hstack([...])' and after applying ra_dec_order/_denormalize_sky when applicable), add an early-return when xy.shape[0] == 0 that returns empty arrays shaped like the broadcasted axes instead of calling func(xy, origin). Specifically:
- Determine out_shape = axes[0].shape (the broadcasted shape).
- If ra_dec_order and sky == 'output': return (np.empty(out_shape, dtype=float), np.empty(out_shape, dtype=float)) (a tuple of two arrays to match the current RA/DEC special-case return type).
- Else: return a list of empty arrays of length self.naxis, each created with np.empty(out_shape, dtype=float).
proposed_fix.reason: Prevents calling the wcslib C routines with ncoord==0 (which raise InconsistentAxisTypesError); returns values that preserve the current API contract for list-of-arrays calling form (same number/type of returned arrays and same broadcasted shape).

proposed_fix.change:
Before invoking result = func(xy, origin), add a guard for zero-length coordinate arrays: if xy.shape[0] == 0 then return an empty array without calling func. Specifically:
- If ra_dec_order and sky == 'output': return np.empty((0, 2), dtype=float)
- Else: return np.empty((0, self.naxis), dtype=float)
(Keep the existing validation that xy.shape[-1] == self.naxis.)
proposed_fix.reason: Preserves the single-array calling convention (result is an (N, M) array where N==0 and M matches expected number of output columns) and avoids invoking wcslib with ncoord==0.

proposed_fix.change:
After broadcasting axes and building xy (i.e. after 'xy = np.hstack([...])') and after applying the ra_dec_order input denormalization (if any), insert an early-return branch when xy.shape[0] == 0 that returns empty arrays shaped like the broadcasted axes WITHOUT calling func(xy, origin). Specifically:
- Determine out_shape = axes[0].shape (the broadcasted shape).
- If ra_dec_order and sky == 'output', return a tuple (np.empty(out_shape, dtype=float), np.empty(out_shape, dtype=float)) to preserve the (ra, dec) tuple return form.
- Otherwise, return a list of empty arrays, one per expected output column; the expected number of output columns should be chosen to match existing API contracts (see 'gaps' below). A reasonable default is to return self.naxis arrays: [np.empty(out_shape, dtype=float) for _ in range(self.naxis)].
This avoids calling func for ncoord==0 and preserves the list-of-arrays calling convention and output shapes.
proposed_fix.reason: Calling wcslib via func(xy, origin) with xy having zero rows causes the underlying C routine to raise InconsistentAxisTypesError. Short-circuiting here prevents the C call and returns empty arrays shaped consistently with the public API for the list-of-arrays calling style.

proposed_fix.change:
Before calling result = func(xy, origin), check if xy.shape[0] == 0 and if so return an appropriately-shaped empty array without calling func. Specifically:
- If ra_dec_order and sky == 'output': return np.empty((0, 2), dtype=float) because normalized sky outputs are shaped (N, 2).
- Otherwise return np.empty((0, self.naxis), dtype=float).
This must be placed after the existing dimension validation (xy.shape[-1] != self.naxis) and AFTER any ra_dec_order 'denormalize' transformation (so denormalize errors still surface when appropriate), but BEFORE func is invoked.
proposed_fix.reason: Single-array calling style normally returns an (N, M) array; for N==0 we should return an (0, M)-shaped array and avoid invoking wcslib with ncoord==0 which raises an error.

proposed_fix.change: After computing pix0 = self.wcs_world2pix(world, origin), check if pix0.shape[0] == 0 and if so immediately return pix0 (do not proceed into iterative logic that uses reductions like np.sum(..., axis=1) or np.nanmax on empty arrays).
proposed_fix.reason: If WCS._array_converter is changed to return empty arrays for zero-length inputs, _all_world2pix may receive empty pix0 and then perform reductions that raise on empty arrays; an early-return prevents invalid reductions and preserves expected output (empty array).

gaps.issue: The original code computes the number of outputs from output.shape[1] after calling func. If short-circuiting and not calling func, code must decide how many output arrays to return; using self.naxis as a fallback may be correct in most cases but could be wrong if any func passed to _array_converter returns a different number of columns.
gaps.needed_change: Ensure the early-return infers the number of output columns correctly for every func that may be passed into _array_converter; in practice, audit each caller (self._all_pix2world, lambdas wrapping self.wcs.p2s/self.wcs.s2p, self._pix2foc, self._p4_pix2foc, self._det2im, self.sip.pix2foc/self.sip.foc2pix) and confirm that the output width equals self.naxis (or 2 for SIP/ra_dec special-case). If any do not, add per-func logic to return the correct number of empty arrays without calling func.

gaps.issue: The method performs axis-1 reductions (np.sum(..., axis=1), np.nanmax(dn)) that assume at least one input point; if pix0 is empty and code continues, these operations will raise ValueError on zero-size arrays.
gaps.needed_change: Add an explicit check for pix0.shape[0] == 0 and return pix0 immediately; audit the rest of the function (adaptive branch, divergence detection, final checks) to ensure they correctly handle empty inputs if reached.

gaps.location: tests and code that call wcslib low-level methods directly (example: astropy/modeling/tests/test_projections.py)
gaps.issue: Some code/test call the low-level Wcsprm methods (w.wcs.p2s / w.wcs.s2p) directly, bypassing WCS._array_converter. Those direct calls will still pass empty arrays to the C extension and will continue to raise InconsistentAxisTypesError for ncoord==0.
gaps.needed_change: Either update those callers/tests to use the public WCS methods (which will be guarded by the _array_converter change) or add Python-side guards/wrappers at those direct call sites (or modify the C extension to accept ncoord==0). List of known direct-call locations found: astropy/modeling/tests/test_projections.py (several calls).

gaps.issue: ra_dec_order special-case: the public API returns different shapes/types (tuple of arrays vs. single Nx2 array) depending on calling style and flags. The early-return must preserve these exact distinctions carefully (tuple vs list vs ndarray) to maintain backward compatibility.
gaps.needed_change: Implement early-return branches that exactly mirror the runtime return types currently produced in the non-empty case: for list-of-arrays calling style + ra_dec_order & sky=='output' return a tuple of two arrays shaped like axes[0].shape; for single-array calling style + ra_dec_order & sky=='output' return an empty array shaped (0,2); for naxis==1 special-case (single-argument 1-D routed to list-of-arrays), ensure returning a list with one empty array shaped axes[0].shape.

gaps.issue: Other methods in WCS that perform reductions on transformation outputs might assume non-empty inputs. I found the critical reductions in _all_world2pix; other similar uses should be checked for handling of zero-length inputs.
gaps.needed_change: Audit WCS methods for any uses of np.max/nanmax/argmax/np.sum (axis reductions) operating on arrays derived from transformation outputs and add early-empty checks or safe-handling where necessary. _all_world2pix is the primary one requiring immediate fix.

gaps.issue: Broadcasted axes shapes must be preserved when returning empty arrays (axes[0].shape may be multidimensional with a zero-length dimension). A naive early return yielding 1-D empty arrays of shape (0,) would break callers expecting broadcasted shapes (e.g., callers that passed higher-dimension broadcasting arrays).
gaps.needed_change: Use axes[0].shape as the return shape for each returned array in the list-of-arrays calling form so that broadcasting shapes (including multi-dimensional shapes with a zero-length dimension) are preserved.

verified_safe.reason: wcs_pix2world calls _array_converter (the change point); after the proposed early-return is implemented, wcs_pix2world will no longer call wcslib for zero-length inputs and will return empty arrays (fixes the bug).

all_pix2world uses _array_converter(self._all_pix2world, ...). The early-return in _array_converter will short-circuit zero-length inputs and return properly-shaped empty arrays; no further changes needed.

wcs_world2pix uses _array_converter to call self.wcs.s2p; with the early-return it will avoid invoking wcslib for zero-length inputs and return an empty array shaped (0, naxis) or (0,2) for ra/dec normalized outputs.

all_world2pix calls _array_converter (wrapping _all_world2pix). The early-return will ensure empty-input behavior is handled consistently; additionally, _all_world2pix internally calls wcs_world2pix/pix2foc which will themselves be safe because they're routed through _array_converter.

All these public methods delegate to _array_converter. The centralized early-return fixes zero-length handling for all of them. Note: sip_pix2foc/sip_foc2pix already short-circuit when self.sip is None; when self.sip is present, they will be protected by the _array_converter change.

These helpers operate via indexing/slicing and allocations based on sky.shape[0]. They will behave correctly on zero-length inputs (slicing on empty arrays returns empty arrays), so they do not need changes; the proposed early-return should be placed after denormalize (per the proposed change) or handle denormalize results consistently.

This method uses wcs_world2pix/pix2foc; since those are routed through _array_converter and will be made safe for zero-length arrays, _all_world2pix will also behave correctly for zero-length inputs (it will get empty arrays and operate accordingly).

This method delegates to _array_converter which will short-circuit zero-length inputs; returning an empty output shaped per calling convention avoids calling wcslib and matches the public API contract for pixel->world transforms.
Also delegates to _array_converter; with the early-return, it will return empty outputs for zero-length inputs without calling wcslib, preserving behavior and shape expectations.
Delegates to _array_converter and will likewise be protected by early-return for zero-length inputs and return an empty (0, naxis) array (or appropriate normalized form).

All of these methods call _array_converter; the early-return there will prevent calling lower-level code with zero-length inputs and will return empty arrays matching the calling convention, which preserves existing API shapes. The SIP 'absent' branches that return input directly already handle empty inputs safely.

These functions operate on the second axis and produce outputs whose first dimension is the number of points; numpy operations on arrays with shape (0, N) are valid and the functions create outputs with shapes (0, N) or (0, 2) as needed, so they are compatible with the early-return approach when placed correctly relative to the short-circuit checks.

This helper operates on the array's first axis (points dimension) and uses numpy slicing; it will work correctly for zero-length arrays and will return arrays with shape (0, naxis) or (0,2) as appropriate, so it is safe to call (or to avoid calling) in the zero-length path and to rely on it for returned shape logic.

This helper also operates on array shapes and returns arrays shaped (N,2) or (N,naxis) as required; with N==0 it returns correct empty shapes (e.g., np.empty((0,2))). So using it in post-processing of early-returns is safe and preserves shape contracts.

Docstrings confirm that core transformations return arrays of shape (ncoord, nelem) and that nelem corresponds to the number of coordinate elements (normally self.naxis); therefore using self.naxis as the expected number of output columns when short-circuiting is consistent with the documented behavior for the core transforms.

When self.sip is None the SIP helper short-circuits and returns input directly (no call into C extension). This existing short-circuit behavior already avoids any wcslib call for that case; no change required for this SIP-absent early-exit path.

bug_report.Title: WCS transformations raise InconsistentAxisTypesError for zero-length inputs
bug_report.Description: High-level WCS transformation methods (e.g. wcs_pix2world) raise an InconsistentAxisTypesError when called with empty coordinate inputs (zero-length lists/arrays). Traceback shows the error originates from the underlying wcslib C routines (wcsp2s / wcsp.s2p) because WCS._array_converter currently always calls into the C extension even when the prepared coordinate array has zero rows. The user-visible behavior should instead be to return empty arrays/lists shaped consistently with the broadcasted input axes and the public API calling style.
bug_report.RootCause: WCS._array_converter's internal dispatch helpers (_return_list_of_arrays and _return_single_array) always call func(xy, origin) after preparing xy, even when xy has zero rows (xy.shape[0] == 0). Passing ncoord == 0 into the wcslib C routines triggers wcslib to raise InconsistentAxisTypesError. There is no short-circuit to return appropriately-shaped empty outputs in the Python layer.
StepsToReproduce: Observed: InconsistentAxisTypesError raised from wcslib (ncoord==0). Expected: empty outputs.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: When called with zero-length coordinate inputs, WCS transformation methods should return empty arrays (or lists/tuples of empty arrays) whose shapes match the broadcasted input shapes (for the separate-axis/list-of-arrays calling style) or (0, M) where M is the number of output columns (for the single-array calling style). For the ra/dec special-case, preserve the existing distinction between list-of-arrays (tuple of two arrays) and single-array (Nx2 array) return conventions.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Calling wcs.wcs_pix2world([], [], 0) raises InconsistentAxisTypesError coming from wcslib because the Python dispatcher calls the C extension with ncoord == 0.
bug_report.Suggestions: Implement early-return guards in WCS._array_converter (both list-of-arrays and single-array calling styles) to detect xy.shape[0] == 0 and return appropriately-shaped empty outputs without calling func(xy, origin). Also add an early-return in WCS._all_world2pix to handle receiving an empty pix0 from wcs_world2pix to avoid empty-array reductions. Update/add tests covering zero-length inputs for all public transformation methods. Audit any code/tests that call low-level wcslib methods directly (e.g. w.wcs.p2s / w.wcs.s2p) and update them to use the public WCS dispatchers or add guards, because those direct calls still pass zero-length arrays directly to the C extension and will still raise.
bug_report.possible_fix: Add short-circuit handling for zero-length coordinate inputs in WCS._array_converter (both _return_list_of_arrays and _return_single_array) so that func is not invoked with ncoord == 0. Return empty arrays shaped using the broadcasted axes shape (axes[0].shape) for list-of-arrays calling style. For single-array calling style return arrays shaped (0, M) where M is self.naxis (or (0, 2) for ra/dec normalized output). Also add an early-return to WCS._all_world2pix: if pix0.shape[0] == 0 return pix0 immediately to avoid reductions on empty arrays. Note: for list-of-arrays early-return the number of output columns is defaulted to self.naxis; this preserves the common contract and the ra/dec special-case. Callers that expect a different number of output columns (rare) should be audited/updated to call via WCS (so the dispatcher can handle the zero-length case) or special-case zero-length inputs themselves.

possible_fix_code.WCS._all_world2pix:
def _all_world2pix(self, world, origin, tolerance, maxiter, adaptive,
                   detect_divergence, quiet):
    # ############################################################
    # #          DESCRIPTION OF THE NUMERICAL METHOD            ##
    # ############################################################
    # (docstring/comments omitted here for brevity in this snippet but
    #  preserved in the real source)

    # initial approximation (linear WCS based only)
    pix0 = self.wcs_world2pix(world, origin)

    # Short-circuit: if there are zero input points, return immediately
    # to avoid reductions (np.sum/np.nanmax) on empty arrays later.
    if pix0.shape[0] == 0:
        return pix0

    # Check that an iterative solution is required at all
    # (when any of the non-CD-matrix-based corrections are
    # present). If not required return the initial
    # approximation (pix0).
    if self.sip is None and \
       self.cpdis1 is None and self.cpdis2 is None and \
       self.det2im1 is None and self.det2im2 is None:
        # No non-WCS corrections detected so
        # simply return initial approximation:
        return pix0

    pix = pix0.copy()  # 0-order solution

    # initial correction:
    dpix = self.pix2foc(pix, origin) - pix0

    # Update initial solution:
    pix -= dpix

    # Norm (L2) squared of the correction:
    dn = np.sum(dpix*dpix, axis=1)
    dnprev = dn.copy()  # if adaptive else dn
    tol2 = tolerance**2

    # Prepare for iterative process
    k = 1
    ind = None
    inddiv = None

    # Turn off numpy runtime warnings for 'invalid' and 'over':
    old_invalid = np.geterr()['invalid']
    old_over = np.geterr()['over']
    np.seterr(invalid='ignore', over='ignore')

    # ############################################################
    # #                NON-ADAPTIVE ITERATIONS:                 ##
    # ############################################################
    if not adaptive:
        # Fixed-point iterations:
        while (np.nanmax(dn) >= tol2 and k < maxiter):
            # Find correction to the previous solution:
            dpix = self.pix2foc(pix, origin) - pix0

            # Compute norm (L2) squared of the correction:
            dn = np.sum(dpix*dpix, axis=1)

            # Check for divergence (we do this in two stages
            # to optimize performance for the most common
            # scenario when successive approximations converge):
            if detect_divergence:
                divergent = (dn >= dnprev)
                if np.any(divergent):
                    # Find solutions that have not yet converged:
                    slowconv = (dn >= tol2)
                    inddiv, = np.where(divergent & slowconv)

                    if inddiv.shape[0] > 0:
                        # Update indices of elements that
                        # still need correction:
                        conv = (dn < dnprev)
                        iconv = np.where(conv)

                        # Apply correction:
                        dpixgood = dpix[iconv]
                        pix[iconv] -= dpixgood
                        dpix[iconv] = dpixgood

                        # For the next iteration choose
                        # non-divergent points that have not yet
                        # converged to the requested accuracy:
                        ind, = np.where(slowconv & conv)
                        pix0 = pix0[ind]
                        dnprev[ind] = dn[ind]
                        k += 1

                        # Switch to adaptive iterations:
                        adaptive = True
                        break
                # Save current correction magnitudes for later:
                dnprev = dn

            # Apply correction:
            pix -= dpix
            k += 1

    # ############################################################
    # #                  ADAPTIVE ITERATIONS:                   ##
    # ############################################################
    if adaptive:
        if ind is None:
            ind, = np.where(np.isfinite(pix).all(axis=1))
            pix0 = pix0[ind]

        # "Adaptive" fixed-point iterations:
        while (ind.shape[0] > 0 and k < maxiter):
            # Find correction to the previous solution:
            dpixnew = self.pix2foc(pix[ind], origin) - pix0

            # Compute norm (L2) of the correction:
            dnnew = np.sum(np.square(dpixnew), axis=1)

            # Bookeeping of corrections:
            dnprev[ind] = dn[ind].copy()
            dn[ind] = dnnew

            if detect_divergence:
                # Find indices of pixels that are converging:
                conv = (dnnew < dnprev[ind])
                iconv = np.where(conv)
                iiconv = ind[iconv]

                # Apply correction:
                dpixgood = dpixnew[iconv]
                pix[iiconv] -= dpixgood
                dpix[iiconv] = dpixgood

                # Find indices of solutions that have not yet
                # converged to the requested accuracy
                # AND that do not diverge:
                subind, = np.where((dnnew >= tol2) & conv)

            else:
                # Apply correction:
                pix[ind] -= dpixnew
                dpix[ind] = dpixnew

                # Find indices of solutions that have not yet
                # converged to the requested accuracy:
                subind, = np.where(dnnew >= tol2)

            # Choose solutions that need more iterations:
            ind = ind[subind]
            pix0 = pix0[subind]

            k += 1

    # ############################################################
    # #         FINAL DETECTION OF INVALID, DIVERGING,          ##
    # #         AND FAILED-TO-CONVERGE POINTS                   ##
    # ############################################################
    # Identify diverging and/or invalid points:
    invalid = ((~np.all(np.isfinite(pix), axis=1)) &
               (np.all(np.isfinite(world), axis=1)))

    # When detect_divergence==False, dnprev is outdated
    # (it is the norm of the very first correction).
    # Still better than nothing...
    inddiv, = np.where(((dn >= tol2) & (dn >= dnprev)) | invalid)
    if inddiv.shape[0] == 0:
        inddiv = None

    # Identify points that did not converge within 'maxiter'
    # iterations:
    if k >= maxiter:
        ind, = np.where((dn >= tol2) & (dn < dnprev) & (~invalid))
        if ind.shape[0] == 0:
            ind = None
    else:
        ind = None

    # Restore previous numpy error settings:
    np.seterr(invalid=old_invalid, over=old_over)

    # ############################################################
    # #  RAISE EXCEPTION IF DIVERGING OR TOO SLOWLY CONVERGING  ##
    # #  DATA POINTS HAVE BEEN DETECTED:                        ##
    # ############################################################
    if (ind is not None or inddiv is not None) and not quiet:
        if inddiv is None:
            raise NoConvergence(
                "'WCS.all_world2pix' failed to "
                "converge to the requested accuracy after {:d} "
                "iterations.".format(k), best_solution=pix,
                accuracy=np.abs(dpix), niter=k,
                slow_conv=ind, divergent=None)
        else:
            raise NoConvergence(
                "'WCS.all_world2pix' failed to "
                "converge to the requested accuracy.\n"
                "After {0:d} iterations, the solution is diverging "
                "at least for one input point."
                .format(k), best_solution=pix,
                accuracy=np.abs(dpix), niter=k,
                slow_conv=ind, divergent=inddiv)

    return pix
